proposals.title: Dreamer-D: World Model-Based Policy Optimization with Deterministic Exploration
proposals.motivation: Model-based RL agents like DreamerV3 use stochastic latent world models for robust planning but often rely on stochastic policies for exploration, which can be inefficient in long-horizon, sparse-reward tasks. The common assumption that deterministic policies inherently limit exploration remains a significant, under-examined bottleneck for scaling world model agents to more complex, open-ended environments where directed, temporally coherent exploration is crucial.
proposals.hypothesis: A deterministic policy, when coupled with a learned world model and a principled exploration objective derived from model uncertainty or information gain, can achieve superior exploration efficiency and final performance compared to standard stochastic policy baselines in complex, partially observable domains.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture, replacing its stochastic actor with a deterministic one. Exploration will be driven by an intrinsic reward computed from the epistemic uncertainty of the world model's latent dynamics, such as the disagreement of an ensemble or the prediction error of an auxiliary random network distillation (RND) module trained on the model's latent states. The deterministic policy will be optimized via backpropagation through the learned world model to maximize the sum of extrinsic and intrinsic rewards over imagined trajectories.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous empirical evaluation of deterministic policies for exploration within a state-of-the-art world model framework. It will deliver a new agent, Dreamer-D, capable of more directed exploration in challenging benchmarks, and offer theoretical and practical insights into the conditions where deterministic world model control excels.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses model uncertainty for exploration or intrinsic motivation, integrating it directly to drive a deterministic policy within a world model's latent imagination loop is novel. This challenges the entrenched paradigm that effective exploration in model-based RL necessitates action-space stochasticity.

proposals.title: Meta-Adaptive World Models: Fast Task-Specific Dynamics Adaptation via Gradient-Based Meta-Learning
proposals.motivation: World model-based agents like DreamerV3 excel at learning from pixels but require extensive environment interaction to learn a new task's dynamics from scratch. This limits their applicability to settings requiring rapid adaptation, such as few-shot task generalization or continual learning. A method to quickly adapt a pre-trained world model to novel tasks or environmental changes would significantly enhance sample efficiency and practical utility.
proposals.hypothesis: A world model's latent dynamics network, pre-trained on a distribution of tasks, can be meta-learned such that its parameters serve as a strong initialization for rapid, few-shot adaptation to new tasks via gradient updates on limited interaction data.
proposals.method: We propose to extend the DreamerV3 architecture by applying a gradient-based meta-learning algorithm (e.g., MAML or Reptile) to its latent dynamics model (RSSM). The agent will be meta-trained on a diverse suite of tasks (e.g., DMC Remix or Meta-World). The objective is to learn initial dynamics parameters that, after a few steps of gradient descent on a small amount of task-specific transition data, yield an accurate adapted model for planning and policy learning. The policy and value networks will be conditioned on the adapted model's latent state.
proposals.contribution: This work would demonstrate a new paradigm for sample-efficient world model adaptation, enabling faster transfer to new tasks and environments. It would contribute a concrete, scalable method for meta-learning latent dynamics and provide empirical benchmarks for few-shot adaptation in visual control domains.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning has been applied to policies and value functions, its systematic integration into latent world models for visual RL is largely unexplored. This approach differs by meta-learning the core dynamics representation itself, rather than just the downstream controller, for more fundamental and flexible adaptation.

proposals.title: Dreamer to Reality: Domain-Adaptive World Models for Sim-to-Real Transfer
proposals.motivation: Training agents in simulation is efficient and safe, but the reality gap—the discrepancy between simulated and real-world dynamics—severely limits the deployment of learned models. Pre-2026, model-based RL agents like DreamerV3 have shown remarkable sample efficiency and performance across diverse simulated tasks, but transferring their powerful world models to real environments remains a significant, unsolved challenge. This work directly addresses the core bottleneck for applying world model-based agents to real-world robotics and embodied AI.
proposals.hypothesis: A world model (e.g., Dreamer) trained primarily on simulation data can be effectively adapted to a target real-world domain using unsupervised representation alignment on unlabeled real video, enabling successful policy transfer without extensive real-world interaction.
proposals.method: We will train a DreamerV3-style world model (RSSM) on a source simulation domain. For adaptation, we will introduce a lightweight, auxiliary domain-invariant representation module. This module will be trained via contrastive or adversarial objectives on sequences of latent states from both simulated rollouts and unlabeled real-world video, aligning their temporal dynamics. The adapted world model will then be used to plan or train a policy for the real-world task, with minimal fine-tuning on sparse real rewards.
proposals.contribution: This will provide a novel, sample-efficient framework for sim-to-real transfer centered on world model adaptation, moving beyond direct policy or value function transfer. It will demonstrate that the structured latent dynamics of world models are a more effective substrate for domain adaptation than raw pixels or task-specific features, potentially enabling a new class of transferable model-based agents.
proposals.novelty: While domain adaptation for RL exists, applying it specifically to the internal latent dynamics of a world model for sim-to-real transfer is novel. This approach leverages the world model's structured representation of temporal causality, which is more aligned across domains than superficial visual features, offering a principled path to bridge the reality gap.

proposals.title: Scalable World Models for Multi-Task and Meta-Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: Current world model-based agents, such as DreamerV3, excel at learning individual tasks from pixels but require retraining for each new task, limiting their efficiency and generality. Scaling to a single model that can perform and adapt to many diverse tasks is a critical step toward generalist agents. This research addresses the core challenge of multi-task and meta-RL scaling using world models, a promising but underexplored direction pre-2026.
proposals.hypothesis: A single, scalable world model, trained with a novel multi-task objective and a structured latent space, can efficiently learn and generalize across a wide distribution of tasks, enabling fast adaptation to new tasks via in-context meta-learning within its imagination.
proposals.method: We will extend the Dreamer architecture with a task-conditioned latent world model. The model will be trained on a curated suite of diverse environments (e.g., DMC, Meta-World, Atari) using a combination of reconstruction, reward, and task-discrimination losses. A key innovation is a hierarchical latent space separating task-specific and task-agnostic dynamics. For meta-RL, we will implement an imagination-based adaptation mechanism where the model fine-tunes its task belief via gradient steps or attention over a context of recent experience within the predicted world model.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a unified agent capable of learning and adapting to multiple tasks without per-task retraining, demonstrating the scalability of world models. It will provide a framework for sample-efficient multi-task RL and fast meta-RL, moving beyond single-task Dreamer variants. The analysis of the learned latent structure will offer insights into disentangled task representations.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task RL exists, scaling the world model paradigm—which jointly learns dynamics, rewards, and task representations—to this setting is novel. The approach uniquely combines the long-horizon planning of Dreamer with in-context meta-learning inside a learned generative model, a synthesis not fully explored pre-2026.

proposals.title: Policy-Guided Exploration for Robust World Models
proposals.motivation: World models, like those in the Dreamer family, learn from agent experience, but their quality is bottlenecked by the exploration strategy used during data collection. Random or naive exploration often yields uninformative data, leading to models with poor generalization or latent state collapse. A principled method to collect data that is maximally informative for the world model itself remains an open challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: A learned policy can be explicitly optimized to generate trajectories that maximize the learning signal for the world model's components (e.g., the dynamics predictor), creating a virtuous cycle where a better policy leads to a better model, which in turn enables learning a better policy.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 agent with a dual objective. The primary objective remains task reward maximization. The secondary objective is an intrinsic reward for model learning, formulated as the prediction error (e.g., RSSM reconstruction or KL divergence loss) of the world model on visited states. The policy will be trained to maximize a weighted sum of these two rewards. We will investigate mechanisms to dynamically balance the weights, perhaps using the gradient conflict between the two losses, to prevent the policy from degenerating into pure, task-agnostic exploration.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete algorithm for closing the loop between policy and model learning, moving beyond passive data collection. It will demonstrate improved sample efficiency and final task performance in long-horizon, sparse-reward domains by learning more accurate and robust world models. The method will also yield insights into what constitutes 'informative' data for latent dynamics models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses prediction error for exploration or auxiliary tasks, the novelty here is the direct, gradient-based use of the *policy* to *actively optimize* the training data for the *world model's own learning objective*, creating a symbiotic, dual-learning process within a single agent architecture.

proposals.title: Dreamer for Sparse Rewards: Learning World Models from Episodic Success Signals
proposals.motivation: Real-world tasks often provide feedback only upon ultimate success or failure, creating a severe credit assignment problem for reinforcement learning agents. Pre-2026 model-based RL methods like Dreamer rely on dense reward signals to learn effective world models and policies, limiting their applicability to domains like robotics and long-horizon planning where rewards are extremely sparse or delayed. Enabling world model learning from such sparse signals is a critical step toward more autonomous and sample-efficient agents.
proposals.hypothesis: A world model can be effectively trained using only episodic success signals by leveraging unsupervised temporal abstraction and an intrinsic reward derived from the model's own learning progress and state novelty.
proposals.method: We propose to extend the DreamerV3 architecture with a two-phase learning process. First, the agent will learn a world model via unsupervised reconstruction and dynamics prediction on raw observations (e.g., images) in a reward-agnostic manner, building a temporally abstract latent state space. Second, we will train a policy using an intrinsic reward combining model prediction error and latent state visitation counts, encouraging exploration of novel states. This intrinsic reward will be modulated by a sparse, delayed extrinsic reward signal only when the task is solved, using a success-conditioned buffer to prioritize successful trajectories for policy refinement.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate that world model-based agents can learn complex behaviors from only episodic success signals, significantly expanding their applicability. It will provide a novel framework for combining unsupervised representation learning with sparse reinforcement learning, advancing sample efficiency in long-horizon tasks.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses model-based intrinsic motivation or unsupervised pre-training, the integration of a temporally abstract world model with a success-conditioned intrinsic reward mechanism specifically designed for the extreme sparse-reward setting is novel. This approach directly addresses the core challenge of credit assignment over long horizons without reward shaping.

proposals.title: Skill-Centric World Models: Accelerating Complex Skill Acquisition via Compositional Latent Imagination
proposals.motivation: Training agents on complex, long-horizon tasks in the real world is severely bottlenecked by sample inefficiency. While world models like Dreamer have improved efficiency in simulation, scaling to real-world interaction, where data is extremely costly, requires further dramatic reductions in required experience. The challenge is to learn robust, composable skills from mere minutes or hours of real-world interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: By structuring a world model's latent space around temporally extended, reusable skill primitives and employing a hierarchical planner that composes these primitives through imagined rollouts, an agent can learn complex behaviors orders of magnitude faster from limited real interaction than existing monolithic world model approaches.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture with a skill-centric latent dynamics model. The latent state will be factorized into a slow, skill-specific variable and a fast, step-level variable. Skills are defined as latent action sequences learned through a skill prior, encouraging diversity and temporal abstraction. Planning will occur at two levels: a high-level policy selects skills via Cross-Entropy Method planning in the skill latent space, and a low-level policy, conditioned on the selected skill, executes actions using model-predictive control in the imagined fast latent space. The model will be trained end-to-end on a combination of reconstruction, reward, and skill diversity losses.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete architecture and algorithm for skill-based world modeling that significantly boosts sample efficiency for long-horizon tasks. It will demonstrate the viability of learning complex, composable behaviors from drastically limited real-world robot interaction data, advancing the frontier of sample-efficient reinforcement learning toward real-world applicability.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior skill-discovery methods that operate in action space or prior hierarchical RL methods with separate high-level controllers, our approach integrates skill learning directly into the latent dynamics of a world model, enabling efficient long-horizon imagination and planning entirely in a learned, abstract skill space.

proposals.title: Dreamer-Joint: End-to-End Reinforcement Learning with a Jointly Trained World Model and Policy
proposals.motivation: State-of-the-art model-based reinforcement learning (RL) agents, such as the Dreamer family, rely on a two-stage process: first training a world model via prediction, then training a policy within it. This decoupling can lead to suboptimality, as the world model is not explicitly optimized to support the downstream task of policy learning. An end-to-end approach could improve sample efficiency and final performance by aligning the world model's latent dynamics directly with policy gradients.
proposals.hypothesis: Jointly training the world model and policy with shared gradients, using a single objective that balances reconstruction and task reward, will produce a more sample-efficient and performant agent than the standard decoupled Dreamer approach.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture, maintaining its RSSM world model and actor-critic policy. The key modification is to train all components simultaneously via gradient descent on a combined loss: L = L_task(actor, critic) + β * L_dyn(world model). The policy's value gradients will be allowed to flow back through the learned latent dynamics to the world model encoder and transition function. We will experiment with gradient stopping, loss weighting (β), and auxiliary losses (e.g., consistency) to stabilize training.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous empirical comparison between joint and decoupled training in model-based RL. It will yield a new, simpler training paradigm for world-model agents and offer insights into the necessary conditions for stable end-to-end latent dynamics learning. Success would challenge the prevailing assumption that world models must be pre-trained as accurate predictors.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored end-to-end model-based RL, applying this to the high-performing and complex Dreamer architecture—specifically allowing policy gradients to shape the latent dynamics—is novel. It directly tests a core architectural choice in Hafner's prior work.

proposals.title: Latent MPC: Online Planning in Learned World Models for Sample-Efficient Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: World models like Dreamer have shown that learning compact latent dynamics enables efficient, long-horizon reinforcement learning. However, their action selection relies on learned, amortized actor networks, which can be suboptimal and require extensive offline training. Integrating online planning, a hallmark of model-predictive control (MPC), could leverage the world model more directly for improved decision-making, especially in complex, non-stationary, or goal-directed tasks where reactive policies struggle.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating sampling-based MPC for online action selection within the compact latent space of a learned world model will yield more sample-efficient and performant agents than pure amortized policy learning, particularly in tasks requiring precise, long-horizon reasoning.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture by adding an online planning module. For each environment step, the learned world model's latent dynamics and reward predictor will be used as the internal simulation for a sampling-based MPC planner (e.g., Cross-Entropy Method or Model Predictive Path Integral control). The planner will optimize a sequence of latent actions over a finite horizon by rolling out candidate trajectories in the latent space, scoring them with predicted rewards and costs, and executing the first action of the best sequence. The actor network will be retained and trained on the planned trajectories to distill and stabilize learning.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a principled fusion of deep latent world models with classical online planning, providing a new paradigm for model-based RL. It will yield a more robust and flexible agent capable of rapid online adaptation and improved long-horizon performance, advancing sample efficiency and final task performance in complex, pixel-based environments.
proposals.novelty: While prior work combines MPC with learned models, performing planning directly in the learned latent space of a state-of-the-art world model (Dreamer) for end-to-end RL is novel. This avoids the inefficiency of pixel-level planning and the inflexibility of pure amortized policies.

proposals.title: Latent Adversarial Imagination: Modeling Opponent Policies for Multi-Agent Competition
proposals.motivation: Model-based reinforcement learning, particularly Dreamer-style latent world models, has excelled in single-agent and cooperative domains by enabling efficient planning through imagined trajectories. However, scaling these methods to competitive multi-agent environments remains a significant challenge. Success in adversarial settings like robotics, gaming, and strategic simulations requires agents to anticipate and adapt to the learned policies of other strategic actors, a capability not addressed by current world models that treat other agents as part of a passive environment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that extending the latent imagination paradigm to explicitly model the latent policies and belief states of other agents will enable more robust and sample-efficient learning in competitive multi-agent environments. Specifically, we claim that an agent can learn a joint latent world model that factors in the predicted actions of opponents, leading to superior strategic planning.
proposals.method: We propose to extend the DreamerV3 architecture by augmenting the latent state with a dedicated component to model other agents. This component will be trained via a combination of 1) an inverse dynamics loss to predict other agents' actions from latent transitions, and 2) a multi-step action-prediction loss using a recurrent network that conditions on the agent's own imagined future actions. During planning, the agent's actor-critic components will perform latent imagination rollouts that simulate not only environment dynamics but also the evolving policy predictions of opponents, enabling it to optimize its policy against anticipated adversarial strategies.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a scalable model-based RL framework for competitive multi-agent settings, demonstrating improved sample efficiency and final performance over model-free and environment-model-only baselines. It will provide a principled method for integrating theory-of-mind-like reasoning into latent world models, advancing the frontier of multi-agent AI in complex, partially observable domains.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has integrated other-agent modeling in model-free settings or used explicit opponent modeling, the novel integration of learned latent opponent policy models within the Dreamer paradigm for *latent adversarial imagination* is unexplored. This moves beyond treating other agents as stochastic environment noise and enables strategic planning against their anticipated learned behaviors.

proposals.title: Task-Agnostic World Models via Intrinsic Dynamics and Information Bottleneck
proposals.motivation: Current world models, such as Dreamer, often rely on auxiliary reward prediction heads to learn representations useful for downstream tasks. This creates a dependency on task-specific reward signals during pre-training, limiting the generality and reusability of the learned world model. A truly general agent should build a task-agnostic understanding of its environment that can be rapidly specialized to any new goal without requiring reward prediction during foundational learning.
proposals.hypothesis: A world model trained solely on a combination of a multi-step dynamics prediction loss and a compressed information bottleneck objective can learn representations that are as effective for downstream policy learning as those trained with an explicit reward prediction auxiliary task.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture by removing its reward prediction head. The agent will be trained on large, diverse datasets of environment interactions (e.g., from DMLab, Minecraft, or procedural generators) using a loss with two components: 1) a long-horizon latent dynamics prediction loss (e.g., RSSM) to enforce temporal coherence, and 2) a variational information bottleneck (VIB) applied to the encoder to promote compression and discard task-irrelevant details. Downstream task adaptation will be tested using either few-shot reinforcement learning or imitation learning, comparing against a DreamerV3 baseline pre-trained with reward prediction.
proposals.contribution: This work would demonstrate a method for learning more general and reusable world models, decoupling representation learning from specific reward functions. It would provide evidence for the sufficiency of dynamics and information-theoretic principles for building capable agents, moving towards a more modular and scalable approach to foundation models for reinforcement learning.
proposals.novelty: While dynamics prediction and information bottlenecks are known, their combined use as a complete replacement for reward prediction in learning world models for RL is unexplored. This directly challenges a standard design choice in model-based RL and investigates the minimal objectives needed for useful agent representations.

proposals.title: Online World Model Adaptation via Task-Aware Latent Replay and Meta-Learning
proposals.motivation: World model-based agents like DreamerV3 demonstrate strong sample efficiency but are typically trained from scratch or fine-tuned slowly on new tasks. For real-world deployment, agents must adapt quickly to novel situations (e.g., a robot encountering an unseen object). Current online updates to the autoencoder and dynamics model are slow and can cause catastrophic forgetting, limiting rapid adaptation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a world model's latent space can be structured and updated via task-aware meta-learning and selective experience replay to enable rapid, stable online adaptation of its components without forgetting prior knowledge.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture with a task-conditioned latent space. We will employ a context-based meta-learning algorithm (e.g., an extension of PEARL) to learn priors over the autoencoder and dynamics model parameters that facilitate fast online adaptation from limited interaction. To prevent forgetting, we will implement a latent replay buffer storing compressed task-specific experiences, which will be interleaved with new data during online updates to regularize the model.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a method for significantly accelerating the online fine-tuning of world model components, moving towards more agile and sample-efficient lifelong learning agents. It will provide empirical benchmarks on adaptation speed and stability across diverse domains from the DMControl suite and Meta-World.
proposals.novelty: While meta-learning and replay buffers exist, their integrated application for fast online adaptation of a world model's perceptual (autoencoder) and predictive (dynamics) components is novel. This directly addresses the bottleneck of slow latent representation updates in model-based RL during task switching.

proposals.title: Inverse Visual Control: Generating Demonstrations and Plans from Specifications via World Models
proposals.motivation: Current reinforcement learning agents learn to act from visual inputs, but the inverse problem—generating a visual plan or demonstration from a high-level goal—remains a core challenge for explainability and instruction-following. Solving this would enable agents to 'imagine' and communicate task solutions, bridging the gap between abstract human instructions (e.g., language commands) and pixel-level execution. This is critical for developing more interpretable and instructable agents that can collaborate with humans.
proposals.hypothesis: A world model trained for forward prediction and control, such as DreamerV3, can be inverted to generate plausible visual trajectories that satisfy a given high-level specification (e.g., a language goal or a goal image) by planning in its latent space and decoding to pixels. This latent plan will serve as an effective visual demonstration for guiding downstream policies.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture, which learns a latent world model (RSSM) via reconstruction and reward prediction. We will train an inverse model that maps from a goal specification (encoded via a pretrained language model or goal image encoder) to a sequence of latent states that achieve it, using a combination of latent planning (e.g., CEM or gradient-based optimization) and a learned prior. The planned latent trajectory will be decoded into a video demonstration. We will evaluate the approach by using these generated videos as rewards for RL (following Hafner et al.'s 'Video Prediction Models as Rewards' method) and by measuring the success rate of plans in simulated robotics domains.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel method for generating visual plans from abstract goals, directly leveraging the structure of learned world models. It will demonstrate a new use case for world models beyond forward prediction and policy training, advancing their role as general-purpose simulators for reasoning. The resulting framework will offer a pathway for converting language or symbolic commands into actionable, visual task decompositions.
proposals.novelty: While world models are used for planning actions, using them to synthesize *visual demonstrations* from high-level specs inverts their typical use and is largely unexplored. This differs from standard video prediction or text-to-video generation by being grounded in a controllable world model trained for interaction, ensuring plans are dynamically plausible and executable.

proposals.title: DreamerV3 in the Wild: Scaling World Models to Complex, Realistic Simulated Environments
proposals.motivation: Current model-based reinforcement learning agents, like the Dreamer family, have excelled in constrained, low-complexity benchmarks. However, their scalability to visually rich and physically realistic simulations—which are crucial stepping stones toward real-world applicability—remains an open question. Testing these agents in such environments is essential to identify the next bottlenecks in representation learning, world model capacity, and long-horizon planning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the DreamerV3 architecture, when combined with enhanced visual representation learning and scaled model capacity, can effectively learn controllable world models and achieve strong sample-efficient performance in complex, realistic simulation benchmarks where prior methods struggle.
proposals.method: We will adapt and scale the DreamerV3 agent for high-complexity 3D simulators like Habitat 2.0 (for embodied AI) and the Isaac Gym/DManD environments (for robotic manipulation). Key technical adaptations include: 1) integrating a larger, pre-trained visual encoder (e.g., a masked autoencoder) for improved pixel-to-latent mapping, 2) scaling the recurrent state-space model (RSSM) with more latent variables and layers to capture complex dynamics, and 3) employing a hierarchical action space for long-horizon tasks. Training will leverage the agent's world model for latent imagination and planning.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous evaluation of a leading world model agent in demanding, realistic benchmarks, establishing new baselines. It will identify specific failure modes and necessary architectural improvements for scaling to complexity, guiding future research in model-based RL toward real-world readiness.
proposals.novelty: While DreamerV3 is robust across many domains, its systematic application and necessary adaptation for visually and physically complex 3D simulators, moving beyond game-like or simplified settings, is a novel and critical test. The integration of pre-trained visual foundations within the world model loop for this scaling purpose is also underexplored.

proposals.title: Task Inference through World Model Inversion: Discovering Reward Structures from Dynamics
proposals.motivation: World models like DreamerV3 learn rich, compact representations of environment dynamics, enabling efficient planning and policy learning. However, they are typically conditioned on a known, externally provided reward function for a specific downstream task. A truly general agent should be able to autonomously discover what tasks are possible and meaningful within its learned world, moving beyond single-task reward specification. This research addresses the inverse problem: inferring the latent space of potential tasks directly from a pre-trained world model.
proposals.hypothesis: The latent dynamics and representation of a learned world model implicitly encode the structure of possible, temporally extended outcomes. By inverting this model—searching for latent goals that lead to distinct, reachable state distributions—we can systematically infer a manifold of plausible reward functions and corresponding tasks.
proposals.method: We will build upon the DreamerV3 architecture. Given a pre-trained world model (RSSM) and decoder, we will freeze its parameters and train a task inference network that takes a batch of imagined rollouts as input. This network will output a distribution over latent task vectors. We will formulate an information-theoretic objective, maximizing the mutual information between the task vector and the resulting trajectories while minimizing the complexity of the inferred rewards. The feasibility of inferred tasks will be validated by training small, fast reinforcement learning policies (using the world model for planning) to achieve them.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework and practical algorithm for task discovery directly from world models, reducing the need for manual reward engineering. It will demonstrate that world models are not just tools for policy learning but can be queried to understand the intrinsic affordances of an environment. This is a step towards more autonomous agents that can self-generate a curriculum of learnable skills.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses world models for planning given a reward, we propose to invert the process to discover the rewards themselves. This differs from unsupervised skill discovery (which learns policies without explicit tasks) by explicitly inferring a structured, interpretable space of potential task descriptions from the world model's dynamics.

proposals.title: Intrinsic Exploration via Contrastive World Models
proposals.motivation: Current world models for reinforcement learning, such as Dreamer, rely heavily on pixel-level reconstruction objectives. This focus on low-level detail can be computationally expensive and may not yield representations that are optimal for high-level reasoning and exploration. A shift towards self-supervised objectives that capture the underlying dynamics and affordances of an environment could lead to more sample-efficient and semantically meaningful world models, enabling agents to discover novel goals without extrinsic rewards.
proposals.hypothesis: World models trained with contrastive or predictive state objectives, rather than pixel reconstruction, will produce latent spaces where distance metrics correspond to semantically meaningful transitions, enabling more effective intrinsic exploration and goal discovery.
proposals.method: We will build upon the DreamerV3 architecture, replacing its image decoder and reconstruction loss with a contrastive learning objective in the latent space. Specifically, we will employ a temporal contrastive loss (e.g., SimCLR or BYOL adapted for sequences) to learn representations where temporally close states are embedded nearby, while distant or unrelated states are pushed apart. The learned dynamics model will then operate in this contrastive latent space. For exploration, we will define an intrinsic reward based on the prediction error of a random network distillation (RND) module applied to these latent states, or via the disagreement of an ensemble of contrastive world models.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a new paradigm for building world models that prioritizes abstract, task-relevant dynamics over pixel fidelity. It will provide a method for sample-efficient, unsupervised exploration in complex visual domains, validated in benchmarks like DMLab and Minecraft. The resulting agents will be better equipped to autonomously discover and master diverse skills.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning has been used for representation learning, its integration as the core objective for a recurrent world model in RL, replacing the canonical reconstruction loss, is a significant departure. This directly challenges the established Dreamer paradigm to improve exploration, not just policy learning.

proposals.title: Goal-Discovery through Predictive State Disagreement: Model-Free Intrinsic Exploration via Temporal Feature Learning
proposals.motivation: World models like Dreamer enable efficient planning via imagined rollouts but require significant computational overhead for model learning and latent imagination. For pure exploration and goal discovery in complex, high-dimensional environments, this may be excessive. A lightweight, model-free alternative could enable agents to discover diverse, temporally extended skills without the complexity of a full world model, aligning with the push for more sample-efficient and scalable unsupervised reinforcement learning.
proposals.hypothesis: An agent can discover a diverse repertoire of skills and goals by intrinsically rewarding transitions that maximally disagree with a set of temporally predictive features, thereby obviating the need for explicit world model rollouts during exploration.
proposals.method: We propose training an ensemble of linear predictors, each trying to forecast a randomly initialized, fixed target network's features of future observations (e.g., 10 steps ahead). The intrinsic reward is the variance (disagreement) among the ensemble's predictions, encouraging visitation of states where the long-term future is hard to predict. This reward drives a model-free policy (e.g., SAC) to explore. Discovered behaviors are captured by a goal-conditioned skill policy, where goals are defined in the space of the target network's features.
proposals.contribution: This work would provide a principled, model-free algorithm for unsupervised skill discovery that is computationally cheaper than world-model-based approaches. It would demonstrate that predictive temporal disagreement, without explicit rollouts, is a sufficient driver for diverse exploration. The resulting skill repertoire could serve as a foundation for efficient downstream task learning.
proposals.novelty: While disagreement-based exploration exists in model-based RL and for dynamics models, applying it to the long-term prediction of abstract visual features without learning a decodable world model or performing rollouts is novel. It directly challenges the assumed necessity of imagined trajectories for discovering diverse temporal abstractions.

proposals.title: Hierarchical World Models for Unsupervised Long-Horizon Goal Discovery
proposals.motivation: Mastering long-horizon tasks with sparse rewards remains a core challenge in reinforcement learning. Current world model agents like Dreamer excel at learning compact state representations and planning, but struggle to discover and sequence meaningful sub-goals over extended time horizons without explicit task supervision. This limits their autonomy in complex, multi-object environments where the final objective is distant.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical agent, with a high-level policy that sets sub-goals in the learned latent space of a world model and a low-level policy trained to achieve them, can autonomously discover and execute useful skill sequences, leading to more efficient exploration and mastery of long-horizon tasks.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture with a two-level hierarchy. The high-level policy operates at a slower temporal stride, outputting sub-goal vectors in the compact latent state space learned by the world model. A separate, low-level policy is trained with intrinsic rewards from a goal-conditioned video prediction model (inspired by Hafner's VPR) to reach these latent sub-goals. The high-level policy is then trained via the world model's imagined trajectories to maximize task reward, effectively learning to propose sub-goals that facilitate long-term planning.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a unified framework combining the strengths of hierarchical RL for temporal abstraction with the sample efficiency and planning capabilities of world models. It will provide a method for agents to autonomously decompose complex tasks, advancing toward more general and capable reinforcement learning agents that can learn diverse skills from interaction alone.
proposals.novelty: While hierarchical RL and world models are established, their tight integration for *unsupervised* sub-goal discovery in the latent space of a learned world model is novel. This moves beyond using hierarchies for pre-defined skills or manually engineered goal spaces, enabling emergent temporal abstraction grounded in the agent's own perceptual model.

proposals.title: Adversarial World Models: Bridging the Sim-to-Real Gap for Robotic Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in training world models for robotics is the distribution shift between imagined rollouts and real-world dynamics, which degrades policy performance upon deployment. While Dreamer-style agents excel in simulated domains, their application to physical robots is limited by this sim-to-real gap. Adversarial training, proven in unsupervised domain adaptation and GANs, offers a principled framework to minimize this discrepancy directly in the latent space of the world model.
proposals.hypothesis: Integrating an adversarial discriminator into the world model's latent dynamics will force the imagined trajectories to match the statistical distribution of real robot experience, thereby learning a more robust latent world model that significantly improves zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for robotic control tasks.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture by adding a convolutional discriminator network that operates on sequences of latent states from the world model's RSSM. This discriminator is trained adversarially to distinguish between latent trajectories generated by the world model's imagination and those encoded from real robot video data. The world model is then updated with an additional adversarial loss, minimizing the Jensen-Shannon divergence between these distributions, alongside its standard reconstruction and reward prediction losses. The policy will be learned entirely within this adversarially-regularized latent world model.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel world model agent capable of more sample-efficient and robust learning from mixed real and simulated data. It will provide a new benchmark for sim-to-real transfer in visual robotic control, demonstrating improved zero-shot performance on tasks like robotic manipulation. The project will also contribute a methodological framework for applying adversarial consistency losses to latent dynamics models.
proposals.novelty: While GANs and domain adversarial networks are established, their integration as a consistency loss *within* the latent space of a world model-based RL agent, specifically to align imagined and real dynamics for robotics, is a novel synthesis. This moves beyond using adversarial networks only for data generation or high-level domain classification.

proposals.title: Active Inference World Models: Recursive Expected Free Energy for Simultaneous Learning and Planning
proposals.motivation: Current world model agents, like Dreamer, separate model learning from policy optimization, requiring task-specific fine-tuning. This limits sample efficiency and the ability to perform general, curiosity-driven exploration. Integrating sophisticated inference principles from active inference, specifically recursive expected free energy (EFE), could enable agents to learn their generative model parameters while actively planning to resolve uncertainty and pursue goals.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating recursive expected free energy as a unified objective for both perception and action within a world model framework will yield agents that learn more accurate and generalizable world models more sample-efficiently, while exhibiting robust goal-directed and exploratory behavior without separate reward signals.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture by formulating the agent's latent state inference and policy as an active inference process. The core innovation is a novel loss that combines the evidence lower bound (ELBO) for model learning with a recursive EFE calculation for planning. The EFE will be approximated recursively through imagined trajectories from the world model, balancing epistemic value (information gain) and pragmatic value (goal achievement). This unified objective will be optimized end-to-end, adjusting both the generative model (transition, encoder, decoder) and the policy network simultaneously.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a new class of world model-based agents that unify learning and planning under a single active inference objective. It will demonstrate improved sample efficiency in model learning and more robust exploration in sparse-reward or open-ended environments. The project will also provide a practical, scalable bridge between active inference theory and state-of-the-art deep reinforcement learning.
proposals.novelty: While active inference has been explored in simple settings, its recursive EFE has not been successfully integrated with large-scale neural world models for simultaneous parameter learning and policy optimization. This moves beyond using world models solely as reward predictors or planners, making the model itself the object of active, uncertainty-driven inquiry.

proposals.title: Contrastive Video Features as Intrinsic Rewards for Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in reinforcement learning is the design of dense, generalizable reward functions, especially in complex, high-dimensional visual domains. While prior work, such as using video prediction models as reward functions, has shown promise, these models can be computationally expensive and may learn representations that are not optimally aligned with task-relevant features. Contrastive learning methods, like SimCLR and BYOL, have proven highly effective at learning robust, temporally-aware visual representations from video data without explicit reconstruction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that contrastive learning objectives applied to raw video sequences can learn more robust and temporally coherent state representations than generative prediction models, and that these representations can serve as superior features for defining intrinsic reward signals, leading to more sample-efficient and generalizable RL agents.
proposals.method: We will train a convolutional encoder using a temporal contrastive loss (e.g., Temporal Contrastive Learning) on large, unlabeled video datasets from target domains (e.g., robotics, DMControl). This encoder will be frozen and used to extract features for a downstream reward function. Specifically, we will integrate it into the DreamerV3 world model framework, using the cosine distance in the contrastive feature space between consecutive states as an exploration bonus or as the primary reward for goal-conditioned tasks. We will benchmark against the established Video Prediction Model (VPM) reward approach across diverse, long-horizon tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work aims to establish a new, computationally efficient paradigm for deriving visual reward functions, reducing the reliance on pixel-accurate generative models. It will contribute a direct comparison between contrastive and generative representation learning for RL rewards within a unified world-model-based agent architecture, providing empirical evidence for the trade-offs and advantages of each approach.
proposals.novelty: While contrastive learning is established in computer vision, its direct application as a source of intrinsic rewards for model-based RL agents is novel. This approach moves beyond using contrastive losses merely for pretraining to actively shaping the agent's exploration and reward dynamics within the world model loop.

proposals.title: Planning in Latent Imagination with Learned Video Prediction Rewards
proposals.motivation: Model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) promises sample efficiency but often suffers from compounding errors in long-horizon planning. Recent work, such as Dreamer, shows that latent world models can enable effective planning, but scaling to complex, high-dimensional visual observations remains challenging. Concurrently, video prediction models have been successfully used as dense reward functions for RL, indicating they capture semantically meaningful world dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: A video prediction model, trained as a reward model for policy learning, can be repurposed as a robust, long-horizon world model for planning if its latent representations are structured to support accurate multi-step rollouts.
proposals.method: We will extend the framework from 'Video Prediction Models as Rewards for Reinforcement Learning' (ICLR 2023). The core method involves training a stochastic video prediction model (e.g., a masked autoencoder or diffusion model) on large-scale, task-agnostic video data. We will then adapt its latent space for planning by learning a compact action-conditioned transition model within it, using techniques from DreamerV3. Planning will be performed via Cross-Entropy Method (CEM) or learned policy optimization in this latent imagination space, using the prediction model's own reconstruction error or a learned value function as the planning objective.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a unified framework where a single video prediction model serves both as a reward source and a planning world model, reducing the need for separate model training. It will provide a method for sample-efficient, long-horizon visual MBRL that leverages pre-trained video priors. The results will advance understanding of the planning-capable representations within generative video models.
proposals.novelty: While video models have been used for rewards and world models exist for planning, directly using a reward-prediction video model's latent dynamics for long-horizon CEM-style planning is unexplored. The key novelty is the co-design of the representation for both reward inference and accurate multi-step latent rollouts.

proposals.title: Policy-Conditioned Video Generation via Inverse World Models
proposals.motivation: World models like DreamerV3 learn compact state representations and dynamics for efficient agent learning. However, the inverse problem—synthesizing realistic, long-horizon video rollouts directly from a given policy—remains largely unexplored. This capability is crucial for agent interpretability, policy transfer verification, and creating rich synthetic training data without expensive environment interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: A world model's latent dynamics and decoder, when conditioned on the latent actions of a specific policy, can generate coherent, high-fidelity videos of that policy's behavior, effectively inverting the typical planning process.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture by training a policy-conditioned video predictor. The model will ingest a latent state and a policy's action distribution (or sampled action) from the learned latent action space. The transformer-based dynamics predictor will be adapted to roll out future latents conditioned on this policy input, with the image decoder reconstructing frames. We will train on large-scale, multi-domain datasets (e.g., DMLab, Minecraft) used in prior work, employing a combination of reconstruction, latent dynamics, and a discriminator loss to ensure visual realism and temporal consistency.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel paradigm for agent visualization and analysis, providing a direct window into a policy's intended behavior. It will contribute a method for generating unlimited, on-policy synthetic experience, which can be used for safe policy evaluation or data augmentation. The resulting model serves as a powerful diagnostic tool for understanding and comparing learned behaviors.
proposals.novelty: While video prediction models have been used as reward functions, using them to *render* the execution of an arbitrary, already-learned policy is a distinct inverse problem. This moves beyond planning with a world model to *simulating* a specific agent's behavior within it, a capability not demonstrated by prior world model or video generation research.

proposals.title: Adversarial Curiosity: Learning Policies that Maximize World Model Uncertainty
proposals.motivation: World models, such as those in the Dreamer family, learn powerful state representations by predicting future observations. However, agents trained with such models can become over-reliant on their learned dynamics, potentially failing to explore novel states that challenge the model's understanding. This limits open-ended skill acquisition and robustness in non-stationary environments. An agent that actively seeks to confound its own world model could drive more thorough exploration and lead to a more robust internal model.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that training an agent to maximize the uncertainty of a concurrently learned video prediction model will lead to more effective exploration behaviors, resulting in the acquisition of a broader and more robust skill repertoire compared to standard intrinsic motivation or model-based methods.
proposals.method: We will build upon the DreamerV3 architecture, maintaining its core world model (RSSM) and actor-critic components. The key innovation is a dual-objective policy: the agent will maximize the standard task reward while also maximizing the prediction error or entropy of the world model's observation predictor. This will be implemented as an intrinsic reward signal added to the environment reward. The video predictor will be a convolutional decoder trained on the agent's experience, and its per-pixel uncertainty (e.g., variance of a Gaussian output distribution) will be computed and aggregated to form the adversarial curiosity bonus.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel exploration strategy that leverages the agent's own world model as a source of adversarial curiosity. It will provide empirical evidence on whether such self-challenging policies improve exploration and final performance in sparse-reward and procedurally generated environments. The project will also yield insights into the co-evolution of an agent's policy and its internal world model.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses world model accuracy or error as a positive intrinsic reward for exploration, the adversarial approach of explicitly training the policy to *increase* the model's uncertainty is a significant inversion. This creates a dynamic, minimax game between the policy and the predictor, distinct from prior prediction-error or empowerment-based methods.

proposals.title: Latent Ambition: Hierarchical Planning with Non-Interpretable Goals via World Models
proposals.motivation: Hierarchical reinforcement learning (HRL) and goal-conditioned agents often rely on human-interpretable goal spaces (e.g., pixel coordinates, object states) to structure long-horizon planning. This constraint can limit the agent's ability to discover and leverage more abstract, task-efficient goal representations that are not easily described by humans. As world models like DreamerV3 have shown strong performance in learning compact latent dynamics, they offer a foundation for exploring whether purely latent, non-interpretable goals can unlock superior hierarchical planning.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical agent, using a learned world model, can plan more efficiently and achieve higher asymptotic performance by setting and pursuing high-level goals defined directly in the model's non-interpretable latent state space, compared to agents constrained to human-specified or semantically interpretable goal spaces.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture with a two-level hierarchy. A high-level manager will operate at a reduced temporal frequency, outputting a goal vector in the stochastic latent space of the world model. A low-level worker, conditioned on this latent goal, will be trained with intrinsic rewards to minimize the distance between its projected future latent states and the goal, using a contrastive or regression objective. Both levels will be trained end-to-end via straight-through gradients, with the manager's policy optimized using the imagined value estimates from the world model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous empirical evaluation of non-interpretable latent goals for HRL within world models, potentially establishing a new performance benchmark. It will contribute a scalable method for learning hierarchical abstractions directly from interaction, reducing the need for manual goal space engineering.
proposals.novelty: While prior HRL and goal-conditioned work often assumes or enforces interpretability, we explicitly relax this requirement, treating the latent space of a world model as a first-class goal space for hierarchical planning, which is a significant departure from standard practice.

proposals.title: Dreamer from Data: Pre-training World Models and Policies with Offline Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: Model-based reinforcement learning (RL), such as the Dreamer family of agents, achieves remarkable sample efficiency by learning a world model from online interaction. However, its initial learning phase still requires costly environment steps. Concurrently, large-scale offline RL datasets exist for many domains, but methods that leverage them often struggle with complex, high-dimensional observations like video. Combining these approaches could drastically reduce the online interaction needed for agents to master new tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pre-training a latent dynamics world model (like DreamerV3) and a latent-conditioned policy on diverse offline video datasets will provide a rich, transferable prior, enabling rapid online fine-tuning and superior final performance compared to training from scratch.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture. First, we will pre-train the world model (encoder, dynamics, decoder) on large, task-agnostic offline video datasets (e.g., RoboNet, D4RL visual variants) using reconstruction and latent dynamics prediction losses. Simultaneously, we will pre-train an actor-critic policy in the model's latent imagination, using offline RL objectives (e.g., Conservative Q-Learning or Implicit Q-Learning) applied to the latent states and actions from the dataset. This yields a pre-trained world model and policy that can be efficiently fine-tuned with minimal online interaction in a target environment.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a principled framework for initializing model-based RL agents with offline data, significantly boosting sample efficiency. It will provide a new benchmark for visual offline-to-online RL and show how world models can serve as a unifying representation for bridging offline pre-training and online fine-tuning.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses offline data for model-free RL or trains world models from online data, our approach uniquely integrates offline pre-training directly into a state-of-the-art model-based RL agent (Dreamer), pre-training both the world model *and* the policy in latent space for efficient transfer.

proposals.title: Meta-Learning Hierarchical World Models for Fast Task Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Current world model agents like DreamerV3 excel at learning complex behaviors from pixels but require extensive environment interaction for each new task. This is inefficient for real-world applications where agents must rapidly adapt to new objectives or changing environments. Meta-reinforcement learning (meta-RL) offers a framework for learning-to-learn, but scaling it to high-dimensional visual domains with effective temporal abstraction remains a significant challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that meta-learning the parameters of a hierarchical world model's high-level planner will enable rapid few-shot adaptation to new tasks by leveraging shared temporal abstractions learned across a distribution of related environments.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture with a hierarchical latent state, separating fast perceptual dynamics from slower task-relevant abstract states. The high-level policy (planner) that operates on the abstract states will be meta-trained using a gradient-based meta-RL algorithm like MAML or Reptile across a distribution of procedurally generated tasks. The adaptation phase will fine-tune only the high-level planner's parameters using a small number of episodes in a new task, while the learned world model and low-level policy remain fixed.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate sample-efficient few-shot adaptation of visual model-based RL agents to novel tasks. It will contribute a scalable method for meta-learning temporal abstractions, bridging hierarchical world models and meta-RL. The resulting agent will be evaluated on adaptation speed and final performance in diverse domains like DeepMind Control and Crafter.
proposals.novelty: While meta-RL and hierarchical RL are established, applying meta-learning specifically to adapt the high-level planner of a state-of-the-art visual world model (Dreamer) is novel. This approach meta-learns how to quickly reconfigure task-specific temporal abstractions, rather than low-level skills or monolithic policies.

proposals.title: Learning Accurate World Models from Hierarchical Plan Supervision
proposals.motivation: World models, such as those in Dreamer, are crucial for sample-efficient reinforcement learning but often struggle with long-horizon accuracy and compounding error. Conversely, hierarchical planning methods can solve complex, long-term tasks by decomposing them, yet they typically rely on or learn a separate, often imperfect, world model. This creates a circular problem: an inaccurate world model leads to poor plans, and poor plans provide weak supervision for learning the model.
proposals.hypothesis: That successful hierarchical plans, which inherently encode temporally extended and causally consistent sequences of subgoals and actions, provide a powerful, structured form of supervision that can significantly improve the long-horizon predictive accuracy and temporal abstraction capabilities of a learned world model.
proposals.method: We propose a dual learning framework where a high-level planner (e.g., a skill or option selector) and a low-level world model (based on the Recurrent State-Space Model of DreamerV3) are co-trained. The planner generates hierarchical plans (sequences of subgoals/skills) to solve tasks. Successful trajectories from executed plans are then used as additional supervision for the world model. Specifically, we will train the world model not only on standard one-step reconstruction and reward prediction losses but also on a novel consistency loss that aligns the model's imagined latent trajectories with the abstract subgoal sequence of the successful plan, enforcing multi-step causal constraints.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a novel method for bootstrapping world model accuracy from high-level reasoning, leading to more robust and temporally coherent models. It will provide a unified framework that closes the loop between planning and model learning, improving sample efficiency and long-horizon task performance in diverse domains.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses world models to *enable* planning, we invert this relationship by using the *output* of a planner to *supervise* the world model. This explicit use of successful hierarchical plans as a multi-step constraint on the latent dynamics is a distinct and unexplored form of model learning signal.

proposals.title: Grounded Symbolic Query (GSQ): Evaluating Emergent Abstraction from Pure Visual Input in Crafter
proposals.motivation: A core challenge in AI is building agents that learn abstract, human-interpretable world models from raw sensory data, a hallmark of human cognition. Prior work on world models and Dreamer agents demonstrates learning from pixels, but evaluating the emergent abstractions within these models remains informal and qualitative. This project addresses the need for a rigorous, quantitative benchmark to measure the emergence of symbolic reasoning grounded purely in visual experience.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that agents trained with reinforcement learning and world model prediction on pure visual input in complex, structured environments like Crafter will develop internal representations that can be queried to produce accurate, symbolic answers about objects, properties, and relations, without ever receiving symbolic input during training.
proposals.method: We will train a DreamerV3 agent on the Crafter environment using only pixel observations and a sparse survival reward. Separately, we will train a lightweight, queryable 'symbolic readout' module that takes the agent's learned latent state as input. This module will be trained via supervised learning on a fixed set of symbolic queries (e.g., 'Do you have wood?', 'Is the zombie to your left?') and their ground-truth answers, which are only used during evaluation phases. The agent's performance will be measured by the accuracy of its symbolic answers, providing a direct metric for grounded abstraction.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce: 1) The GSQ benchmark, a novel evaluation protocol for measuring emergent symbolic abstraction in visually grounded agents. 2) Empirical evidence on the conditions under which useful abstractions emerge from pure interaction. 3) A step towards more interpretable and composable world models learned from pixels.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior evaluation methods, GSQ explicitly decouples the perceptual learning objective from the symbolic evaluation, providing a clean, objective metric. It also differs from neuro-symbolic approaches by not injecting symbolic knowledge during training, testing for truly emergent abstraction.

proposals.title: DreamCoder: Latent Program Synthesis for Long-Horizon Planning and Tool-Crafting
proposals.motivation: Current agents struggle with long-horizon tasks requiring the sequential crafting of tools, as in the Crafter environment, due to the compounding errors of model-based rollouts and the sample inefficiency of pure model-free RL. While Dreamer-style world models enable efficient latent planning, their rollouts degrade over long horizons, and program synthesis offers structured reasoning but is costly to evaluate directly in the environment. A method that synergistically combines these paradigms could unlock efficient, abstract long-term reasoning.
proposals.hypothesis: An agent that uses a program synthesizer to generate high-level action sequences and evaluates them within a latent world model trained with an improved long-horizon objective will achieve superior sample efficiency and performance on complex, multi-stage tool-crafting tasks compared to either approach alone.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture with a latent overshooting objective that minimizes prediction errors not just for the next step, but for a distribution of future latent states over long horizons, improving rollout fidelity. A program synthesizer, inspired by DreamCoder, will generate interpretable action programs as candidate plans. These programs will be compiled into latent action sequences and evaluated via rollouts in the improved world model, with the outcomes used to guide program search and refinement, all within the latent space for efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel agent architecture that tightly integrates neural program synthesis with latent model-based planning. It will demonstrate significant gains in sample efficiency and final performance on long-horizon crafting benchmarks like Crafter, and provide a framework for abstract, program-like reasoning within learned world models.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the closed-loop integration where a program synthesizer is guided not by environment interactions, but by latent simulations from a world model explicitly trained for accurate long-horizon predictions, creating a new paradigm for abstract, sample-efficient planning.

proposals.title: Learning Symbolic Abstractions for Hierarchical Planning and Grounding in Crafter
proposals.motivation: Current model-based agents like DreamerV3 excel at learning from pixels but struggle with long-horizon reasoning and planning. Conversely, symbolic planning is efficient for reasoning but requires handcrafted abstractions, limiting scalability. Automatically learning a minimal, grounded symbolic representation from agent experience could bridge this gap, enabling efficient high-level reasoning while retaining the robustness of low-level pixel control.
proposals.hypothesis: An agent can co-learn a predicate-based symbolic abstraction of the Crafter environment and a hierarchical policy that uses this scaffold for planning, leading to more sample-efficient mastery of long-horizon tasks compared to purely pixel-based world models.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture with a trajectory abstraction module that clusters latent states and actions from successful episodes into symbolic predicates (e.g., Has(wood), At(forest)). This defines a PDDL-like planning domain. A high-level planner (using a classical solver or a learned policy) operates on this symbolic space to generate subgoals. A low-level Dreamer-based controller, conditioned on the current subgoal, executes in the pixel space. The symbolic scaffold is continuously refined from new agent trajectories.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a novel method for bootstrapping symbolic reasoning from grounded experience without manual engineering. It will provide a concrete benchmark for neuro-symbolic integration in Crafter and yield insights into the emergence of abstract concepts in world models.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior neuro-symbolic work that often uses fixed symbols, our key novelty is the automated, continual learning of the symbolic abstraction directly from the agent's latent world model and experience, creating a tight, adaptive coupling between abstract planning and grounded execution.

proposals.title: Intrinsic Control via Temporally Abstract World Models
proposals.motivation: Current world model-based agents, like DreamerV3, rely on predicting explicit task rewards, which limits their autonomy and ability to discover useful behaviors in the absence of a dense reward signal. This reliance is a bottleneck for open-ended learning and deployment in complex environments where reward functions are sparse, costly to specify, or non-stationary. Developing agents that can learn useful behaviors without explicit reward prediction is a core challenge for advancing generalist AI.
proposals.hypothesis: A world model trained solely on unsupervised prediction objectives can learn a temporally abstract latent skill space, and an intrinsic objective based on empowerment or skill diversity within this space can drive the discovery of a wide repertoire of useful, controllable behaviors without any reward prediction.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture by removing its reward predictor. The world model will be trained on pixel and language observations using reconstruction and contrastive losses. We will learn a discrete, temporally extended latent skill variable that modulates the model's transition dynamics. An intrinsic policy will be trained via reinforcement learning to maximize the empowerment of these skills—the mutual information between skills and future latent states—or to maximize their diversity using a discriminability objective, all computed using the world model's representations.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a new class of world model agents that acquire rich behavioral repertoires through pure unsupervised interaction, advancing the field of intrinsic motivation. It will provide a principled framework for pre-training generally capable agents that can later be efficiently fine-tuned with downstream rewards.
proposals.novelty: While prior work on empowerment and unsupervised skill discovery exists, integrating it into a state-of-the-art, long-horizon world model framework (like Dreamer) to replace reward prediction entirely is a novel synthesis. The key innovation is using the world model not just for prediction but to directly define and optimize a temporally abstract intrinsic objective for control.

proposals.title: DreamerV2 for Robotic Control: Scaling World Models to Continuous Action Spaces
proposals.motivation: Model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) via world models, such as DreamerV2, has shown remarkable sample efficiency and performance on discrete-action domains like Atari and DeepMind Control Suite. However, its application to high-dimensional, continuous control tasks—particularly in robotics—remains underexplored. Demonstrating DreamerV2's efficacy on complex robotic manipulation and locomotion benchmarks is crucial for advancing sample-efficient, general-purpose agents for real-world interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: The DreamerV2 agent, with its learned latent world model and actor-critic policy, can achieve state-of-the-art sample efficiency and asymptotic performance on standard continuous control benchmarks, outperforming both model-free algorithms and prior model-based approaches that do not leverage latent imagination.
proposals.method: We will adapt the DreamerV2 architecture, which uses a Recurrent State-Space Model (RSSM) for world modeling and trains an actor and critic entirely in latent space via analytic gradients. The core implementation will be tested on continuous-action tasks from the DeepMind Control Suite (e.g., `walker_run`, `manipulator_insert_ball`) and the Meta-World robotic manipulation benchmark. We will conduct systematic comparisons against strong baselines, including SAC (model-free) and PlaNet (model-based), measuring sample efficiency and final performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive evaluation of DreamerV2 on high-dimensional continuous control, establishing a new performance benchmark for world model-based agents in robotics. It will yield insights into the scalability of latent imagination and identify potential failure modes or necessary architectural adjustments for complex, contact-rich tasks.
proposals.novelty: While DreamerV2 has been validated on domains with discrete or simple continuous actions, its application to complex robotic tasks with high-dimensional action spaces and sparse rewards is a non-trivial extension. The study will explicitly test the limits of its world model's generalization and policy learning in these challenging, physically-grounded settings.

proposals.title: Scaling Laws for World Models: Investigating the Impact of Massive Compute on Model-Based Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: Recent work, such as DreamerV3, has demonstrated that world models trained via reconstruction and prediction can enable highly data-efficient and performant agents. However, the scaling properties of these models with respect to computational budget remain largely unexplored, unlike the well-documented scaling laws for large language models. Understanding these properties is crucial for guiding future resource allocation and unlocking new capabilities in model-based RL.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that scaling world model training with significantly larger computational budgets—applied to model size, dataset size, and training duration—will yield predictable improvements in both the accuracy of learned dynamics and, consequently, the final task performance of the resulting agents.
proposals.method: We will conduct a systematic scaling study using the DreamerV3 architecture as a baseline. We will scale key axes: model capacity (transformer or CNN-RNN hybrid parameters), the diversity and quantity of pre-training environment interaction data, and training iterations. Performance will be evaluated on a diverse suite of tasks from the DeepMind Control Suite and Atari, measuring both world model prediction metrics (e.g., PSNR, KL) and the final agent's sample efficiency and asymptotic reward.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first empirical scaling laws for world models in RL, providing a quantitative framework for predicting performance gains from increased compute. It will identify potential bottlenecks and reveal whether scaling primarily improves model fidelity, generalization, or exploration capabilities.
proposals.novelty: While scaling laws are established in language and vision, their systematic investigation in the context of temporal, latent-variable world models for control is novel. This moves beyond simply applying more compute to understanding its predictable, quantifiable impact on a core component of model-based RL.

proposals.title: DreamerV3 with Augmented Views: Improving World Model Robustness via Contrastive Data Augmentation
proposals.motivation: World models like DreamerV3 learn powerful state representations from pixels, but their visual encoders can overfit to superficial pixel statistics, limiting generalization to unseen environments. In contrast, contrastive learning methods like SimCLR and BYOL have demonstrated that strong, task-agnostic data augmentations (e.g., random cropping, color jitter, Gaussian blur) are crucial for learning robust visual representations. Integrating these augmentation strategies into world model training could yield more invariant and informative latent states, improving sample efficiency and final task performance in reinforcement learning.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying the strong, diverse data augmentations developed for contrastive self-supervised learning to the visual observations used to train a world model (e.g., DreamerV3) will lead to more robust and generalizable latent state representations, which in turn will improve the agent's sample efficiency and asymptotic performance on downstream tasks.
proposals.method: We will integrate a stochastic data augmentation module into the DreamerV3 architecture, applying augmentations like random convolutions, small-patch cropping, and color jitter to the input image observations before they are encoded.
