The world model will be trained to predict the latent dynamics and rewards from these augmented views. Crucially, we will investigate two training regimes: 1) using the same augmentation for the input and reconstruction target within a prediction step, and 2) using different augmentations to enforce temporal consistency across views. We will benchmark performance against standard DreamerV3 on the DMLab and Atari benchmark suites, measuring sample efficiency and final score.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic study of how contrastive-style augmentations can be adapted for sequential world model learning, potentially establishing a new standard for visual representation learning in model-based RL. It will deliver a more robust variant of DreamerV3 with improved generalization, validated across diverse visual domains, and contribute insights into the interplay between augmentation invariance and temporal predictive learning.
proposals.novelty: While data augmentation is common in computer vision, its application in world models has been limited to simple techniques like frame stacking. This proposal systematically adapts the strong, multi-view augmentations from contrastive learning—designed for static images—into the sequential, generative framework of a world model, which is a non-trivial integration due to the need for temporal coherence.

proposals.title: Unsupervised Goal Discovery via Latent World Model Divergence
proposals.motivation: A core bottleneck in reinforcement learning is the need for manually specified reward functions, which limits scalability and autonomy. Pre-2026 world models, such as Dreamer, have shown remarkable success in learning compact latent dynamics from pixels, but they still require external reward signals for planning. This project aims to move beyond this limitation by enabling agents to infer their own goals directly from their learned model of the world.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a world model, trained purely on passive observation and agent interaction, can autonomously identify and represent salient, controllable, and temporally extended outcomes in its latent state space, and that these representations can serve as effective intrinsic reward signals for downstream skill learning.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture with a contrastive learning objective that operates on the latent dynamics. Specifically, we will train an ensemble of world models on different temporal segments of a large, unlabeled interaction dataset. The divergence in next-step latent predictions between ensemble members will be used to identify states where the world model is uncertain or surprised, indicating potential causal bottlenecks or goal states. These states will be clustered into a discrete set of discovered goals. A separate policy will then be trained using the Dreamer framework, but with a reward defined as minimizing the distance to the latent representation of a sampled discovered goal.
proposals.contribution: This work would contribute a principled, unsupervised method for goal and reward discovery grounded directly in an agent's world model. It would demonstrate the feasibility of learning complex behaviors without any task-specific reward engineering, advancing the field toward more general and autonomous agents. The resulting 'goal library' could be a reusable substrate for hierarchical reinforcement learning.
proposals.novelty: While prior intrinsic motivation work uses hand-designed heuristics like curiosity or empowerment, our method derives goals directly from the structure and uncertainty of the learned world model itself, inverting the typical pipeline. This is a non-obvious synthesis of world model ensembles, latent contrastive learning, and goal-conditioned RL without pre-defined rewards.

proposals.title: Preference-Guided World Models: Aligning Model-Based RL with Human Feedback
proposals.motivation: Model-based RL agents like DreamerV3 learn powerful world models from interaction but rely on engineered reward functions, which are often misaligned with complex human goals and values. Integrating human feedback directly into the world model learning process is a promising path toward more capable, general, and aligned agents that can solve tasks defined by human preferences, not just pre-specified rewards.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating human preference or demonstration data into the latent dynamics model of a world model agent will enable more sample-efficient learning of reward functions and policies that are robustly aligned with human intent, outperforming both pure RL and imitation learning in sparse or hard-to-specify reward settings.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture by incorporating a human preference model into its latent space. Using methods inspired by preference-based RL (e.g., PEBBLE) and inverse dynamics modeling, we will train a reward predictor on pairwise human comparisons or sparse demonstrations. This predictor will be trained jointly with the world model, using the imagined trajectories from the world model to regularize and improve the reward model's generalization, and the learned rewards will drive policy learning via latent imagination.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a unified framework for learning world models and reward functions from human feedback, demonstrating improved sample efficiency and alignment on tasks with ambiguous or complex objectives. It will provide a principled method to bootstrap capable agents in environments where reward engineering is infeasible.
proposals.novelty: While prior work combines RL with human feedback or uses world models for pure imitation, the novel integration lies in co-training the preference/reward model *within* the world model's latent dynamics, allowing the agent to use its understanding of the world to interpret and generalize human feedback more effectively.

proposals.title: Collaborative World Models: Scaling Model-Based RL via Distributed Learning and Knowledge Sharing
proposals.motivation: World models, as exemplified by the Dreamer series, are powerful for sample-efficient reinforcement learning but remain computationally expensive to train, especially in complex, high-dimensional environments. Scaling these models often requires prohibitive single-agent compute and data collection. Distributed, multi-agent learning offers a promising path to accelerate training and improve model robustness by aggregating diverse experiences, yet no framework exists for collaboratively learning a shared, predictive world model.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a population of agents, each exploring different parts of a task or environment, can collaboratively learn a more accurate, generalizable, and robust world model faster than a single agent, and that this shared model will accelerate policy learning for all agents.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture to a distributed setting. Multiple agents will collect experience in parallel, each contributing to a shared replay buffer. A central world model, built on a Transformer or RSSM, will be trained on this aggregated data. We will investigate synchronization strategies (e.g., parameter averaging, gradient sharing) and a novel attention-based mechanism for agents to selectively share and query temporally extended experience sequences relevant to their current context, inspired by the retrieval methods in 'Learning to Model the World with Language'.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable framework for training world models, significantly reducing wall-clock training time. It will demonstrate how distributed experience collection improves model generalization and robustness across diverse domains. The resulting algorithm will be a step towards making large-scale, model-based RL more practical and accessible.
proposals.novelty: While distributed RL exists for model-free methods, applying it to the joint, iterative learning of a shared latent dynamics model is novel. The key innovation is the design of communication protocols for sharing and retrieving predictive sequences, moving beyond simple parameter or gradient averaging to structured, experience-based knowledge transfer.

proposals.title: DreamerCraft: Automatic Task Generation via Latent World Model Intervention
proposals.motivation: Current reinforcement learning agents require hand-designed tasks or environments, which limits their scalability and autonomy. A world model, like those in the Dreamer series, learns a compact, controllable representation of environment dynamics. This proposal posits that such a model can be inverted to automatically synthesize novel, learnable tasks, moving beyond passive prediction to active curriculum creation.
proposals.hypothesis: By strategically intervening on the latent states and dynamics of a pre-trained world model, we can generate sequences of imagined states that correspond to novel, feasible, and progressively challenging tasks for an agent to learn, accelerating mastery of complex skills.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture. After training a world model on a broad dataset or environment, we will develop a task generator that operates in the model's latent space. This generator will use a learned or optimization-based process (e.g., latent gradient ascent) to find state trajectories that maximize a task-quality objective, such as the agent's predicted learning progress or the entropy of required skills. The generated latent trajectories will be decoded into goal images or descriptions, forming a curriculum for training a separate policy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, model-based framework for automatic task and curriculum generation, reducing reliance on human engineering. It will demonstrate how world models can be used not just for planning and efficiency, but as a source of structured, adaptive challenge, pushing agents toward broader competence.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses world models for planning or as reward functions, the core novelty is actively inverting the model to *design* tasks, framing task generation as a search/optimization problem within the learned latent dynamics, rather than relying on pre-defined task distributions or random environment resets.

proposals.title: Learning General Dynamics through Goal-Conditioned Deconstruction
proposals.motivation: Learning general, reusable world models from interaction remains a core challenge in AI. Current methods, like Dreamer, often learn dynamics tied to specific reward functions, limiting transfer. This project proposes that an agent can learn a more foundational model of physical causality by focusing on the inverse problem of efficiently deconstructing complex structures, a task with inherent, dense dynamics but no externally imposed goal beyond efficiency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an agent trained with a decoupled objective—learning dynamics purely from deconstruction actions and a separate model to predict deconstruction efficiency—will develop a more general and transferable world model than one trained with a monolithic reward-prediction objective.
proposals.method: We will adapt the DreamerV3 architecture with two decoupled world model components: a deterministic dynamics model that learns object-state transitions from deconstruction actions, and a separate, lightweight 'efficiency critic' that predicts the future rate of resource consumption. The agent will be trained via reinforcement learning in procedurally generated 3D environments (e.g., Minecraft-inspired) where the goal is to maximally disassemble complex block structures. The learned dynamics model will then be frozen and evaluated by fine-tuning only the critic on novel downstream construction and manipulation tasks.
proposals.contribution: This work aims to contribute a method for learning more disentangled and generalizable world dynamics by leveraging the structure of inverse tasks. It would provide evidence for the value of decoupling physical prediction from value estimation during model-based RL pre-training, potentially leading to models with better sample efficiency and transfer in physical reasoning domains.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in framing deconstruction as a self-supervised, goal-conditioned pre-training task for world models and in the architectural decoupling of dynamics from the efficiency-prediction pathway, moving beyond the integrated reward prediction of prior model-based RL.

proposals.title: Decoupled Dynamics Learning for Robust World Models
proposals.motivation: In model-based reinforcement learning (RL), world models like Dreamer learn a joint dynamics-and-reward model. This coupling can lead to dynamics representations that are biased towards predicting reward, potentially harming generalization and sample efficiency in novel or reward-sparse environments. A decoupled approach could yield more general-purpose world models, a core theme in Hafner's research on learning to model the world.
proposals.hypothesis: Learning environment dynamics via an objective independent of reward prediction will produce more robust and generalizable world models, leading to improved zero-shot generalization and adaptation in downstream RL tasks.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture by training two separate latent dynamics models. The primary dynamics model will be trained via a purely self-supervised objective, such as contrastive learning or a multi-step video prediction loss on observations, decoupled from the reward stream. A separate, smaller reward predictor will be trained on top of these dynamics latents. We will evaluate the decoupled world model by using it for planning in the Dreamer framework and benchmarking its performance on task adaptation and zero-shot generalization in the DMControl Suite and XLand, comparing against the standard coupled Dreamer baseline.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide empirical evidence on the benefits and trade-offs of decoupling dynamics from reward in world models. It will contribute a new model-based RL agent with stronger generalization capabilities and a clearer architectural separation for interpretability and transfer.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored auxiliary losses, the explicit architectural and objective decoupling of dynamics from reward prediction within a state-of-the-art world model (Dreamer) is novel. This directly tests the assumption that a single latent model must jointly account for both.

proposals.title: Adversarial Consistency for Stable Long-Horizon World Models
proposals.motivation: World models like Dreamer enable agents to learn by planning in a learned latent state space. However, training these models for long-horizon predictions remains unstable; latent trajectories often diverge from reality, limiting the effective planning horizon and downstream task performance. This instability is a key bottleneck for scaling model-based reinforcement learning to complex, long-term tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that regularizing the latent dynamics model using an adversarial consistency loss, which forces long-horizon imagined trajectories to be indistinguishable from real latent state sequences, will enable stable training of significantly longer prediction horizons and improve the fidelity of the learned world model.
proposals.method: We extend the DreamerV3 architecture with a latent trajectory critic network. This critic is trained to discriminate between sequences of latent states from the model's own multi-step rollouts (latent overshooting) and sequences obtained by encoding real environment trajectories. The dynamics model is then jointly trained to minimize its standard prediction loss and to maximize the critic's error (an adversarial loss), grounding its long-horizon predictions. Training employs a curriculum, gradually increasing the overshooting horizon.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a method for training more stable and accurate long-horizon world models, directly addressing a core limitation in model-based RL. It will demonstrate improved performance on tasks requiring long-term reasoning and offer a new technique for regularizing latent dynamics through adversarial consistency.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial methods have been used for observation-level prediction, applying them to regularize the consistency of latent state trajectories over long horizons is novel. This integrates adversarial grounding directly into the latent overshooting framework, moving beyond simple KL penalties.

proposals.title: Latent Overshooting for Compositional Emergence: Scaling World Model Planning in Crafter
proposals.motivation: Current world model agents like DreamerV3 excel in learning compact latent dynamics but struggle with long-horizon tasks requiring the composition of many primitive skills. Environments like Crafter, where agents must chain actions to build, farm, or craft, reveal this limitation. The field lacks a principled understanding of how to train latent models specifically for extended temporal consistency, which is hypothesized to be the foundation for such emergent, compositional behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly training a world model's latent space for multi-step predictive consistency via advanced latent overshooting objectives will directly enable and improve the emergence of complex, compositional behaviors in long-horizon, sparse-reward environments.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture with a novel latent overshooting objective that maximizes the mutual information between forward and reversed latent trajectories over hundreds of imagined steps. This objective will be integrated as an auxiliary loss during world model training, regularizing the latent dynamics for long-horizon coherence. We will evaluate the agent in the Crafter benchmark, analyzing the correlation between improved latent temporal metrics and the successful execution of chained tasks like tool creation and farming.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct method to improve the long-horizon planning capabilities of world models and a mechanistic analysis linking latent space properties to the emergence of complex behavior. It will advance the understanding of compositional skill learning in model-based reinforcement learning.
proposals.novelty: While latent overshooting exists, its application is framed as a general regularizer. We uniquely target it as the primary driver for emergent compositionality, using the structured complexity of Crafter's behaviors as both the benchmark and the training signal for extreme-horizon latent consistency.

proposals.title: Uncertainty-Aware Arbitration for Robust Model-Based Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: Integrating model-based planning with model-free policies, as in PlaNet, offers sample efficiency but often suffers from destructive interference during training. Poor model predictions early on can corrupt the policy, while an inflexible policy can hinder the planner's exploration. A principled, adaptive mechanism is needed to balance their contributions dynamically based on their current reliability.
proposals.hypothesis: An arbitration module that dynamically blends or selects between model-based planner and model-free policy actions, based on real-time estimates of world model epistemic uncertainty and policy value confidence, will prevent destructive interference and yield more robust and sample-efficient learning.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture, incorporating a PlaNet-style planner for latent imagination. The key addition is an arbitration network that takes as input: 1) the epistemic uncertainty of the world model's next latent state prediction (estimated via an ensemble or prediction error variance), and 2) the confidence of the model-free critic's value estimate (e.g., via distributional RL variance). This network will output a soft blending weight or a discrete selection to combine the planner's and policy's proposed actions. The system will be trained end-to-end, with the arbitrator's gradients encouraging the use of the more reliable component.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a general, learnable framework for mitigating the instability of joint model-based and model-free training. It will demonstrate improved robustness and final performance on a suite of long-horizon, visually complex tasks from the DMControl and Atari benchmarks, while maintaining the sample efficiency of world models.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has combined model-based and model-free components, the introduction of a learned, uncertainty-aware arbitrator that dynamically modulates their influence during both training and deployment is a novel and non-obvious solution to the interference problem.

proposals.title: Arbitrating Between Plans and Habits: A Biologically-Inspired Architecture for Efficient Reinforcement Learning
proposals.motivation: Current reinforcement learning agents excel either at sample-efficient planning with world models (e.g., PlaNet, Dreamer) or at fast, habitual control with model-free policies, but struggle to seamlessly integrate both. This mirrors a fundamental challenge in biological intelligence: balancing deliberative, model-based control with reflexive, model-free action. Achieving such integration in artificial agents is crucial for developing systems that are both data-efficient and capable of real-time, robust decision-making across diverse and complex environments.
proposals.hypothesis: A cognitive architecture that explicitly arbitrates between a model-based planner and a model-free policy—using a gating mechanism informed by state uncertainty and value estimates—will outperform purely model-based or model-free agents in terms of final performance, sample efficiency, and computational cost per action.
proposals.method: We will build upon the DreamerV3 architecture as a core world model and model-based planner. A separate, fast model-free policy network will be trained concurrently. The key innovation is a lightweight gating network, inspired by prefrontal arbitration, that takes as input the planner's uncertainty (e.g., ensemble variance) and the value estimates from both systems. This gate outputs a soft weighting to blend the planned and habitual actions, or selects one outright. The planner will also provide sub-goals to guide the policy's learning, while the policy's experience will refine the world model.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a unified agent architecture that dynamically balances planning and habit, leading to superior trade-offs in sample efficiency and inference-time latency. It will provide a principled, neuroscience-inspired framework for hybrid RL, moving beyond simple ensemble or switching schemes. The results will advance our understanding of how to build more adaptive and efficient autonomous agents.
proposals.novelty: While hybrid RL models exist, the explicit design of an *arbitration mechanism* based on computational principles of cognitive neuroscience (model-based vs. model-free arbitration) is novel. This goes beyond using a model solely for planning or as a reward signal, instead creating a synergistic, interacting system where each component trains and guides the other.

proposals.title: DreamCraft: Hierarchical World Models for Compositional Planning in Vast Procedural Worlds
proposals.motivation: Scaling reinforcement learning to vast, structured environments with long-horizon goals remains a fundamental challenge. Pre-2026, agents excel in narrow domains but struggle with the combinatorial complexity of open-ended worlds where actions must be composed into skills. This research addresses the dual scaling problems of immense state spaces and exponentially large, structured action spaces through hierarchical skill abstraction.
proposals.hypothesis: An agent equipped with a hierarchical world model that learns to predict the outcomes of both primitive actions and learned skill abstractions can discover and plan with compositional skills, enabling efficient exploration and long-horizon task achievement in a vast, procedurally generated crafting world.
proposals.method: We will develop a 'Mega-Crafter' environment with a deep, tree-structured item hierarchy and complex recipes. The agent will be based on the DreamerV3 architecture, augmented with a skill discovery module. This module uses a skill-conditioned RSSM to predict the outcomes of parameterized skill macros, which are sequences of primitives discovered through hindsight relabeling or diversity objectives. Planning will occur at two levels: a high-level planner selects skills using learned value functions, and a low-level planner (or policy) executes the primitive sequence, with both levels trained jointly via latent imagination.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver: 1) a scalable benchmark for hierarchical planning in vast compositional spaces, 2) an algorithm that unifies world model learning with hierarchical skill discovery, and 3) empirical evidence that compositional skill abstraction is critical for scaling to immense open-world environments. It will advance the field toward more general, reusable agents.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (like Dreamer) learns world models and some hierarchical methods discover skills, this tightly integrates skill-conditioned world modeling and hierarchical latent planning specifically for the exponentially large action spaces of structured, compositional environments, moving beyond fixed, flat action sets.

proposals.title: Learning Temporally Abstract World Models for Hierarchical Planning
proposals.motivation: Current model-based RL agents, like Dreamer, learn world models at a fixed, high-frequency timescale, which can make long-horizon planning computationally expensive and brittle. Hierarchical RL offers a promising path for temporal abstraction, but integrating it effectively with learned world models for multi-scale planning remains an open challenge, especially for mastering diverse, long-duration tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical agent with a two-level world model—a high-level model predicting the outcomes of learned temporally extended macro-actions, and a low-level model for fine-grained control—will achieve more sample-efficient learning and superior long-horizon performance in complex environments compared to flat model-based baselines.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture with a hierarchical latent state. A high-level policy, trained with reinforcement learning, will propose goals in the latent space or output abstract actions at a reduced temporal frequency. A low-level policy, conditioned on these goals, will be trained using a combination of reinforcement learning and latent-conditioned video prediction losses to execute the macro-action over multiple steps. The high-level model will be trained to predict the discounted return and the post-macro-action latent state, enabling efficient planning in the abstract space.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a unified framework for learning and planning with temporally abstract world models, directly addressing the long-horizon credit assignment problem. We expect to demonstrate significant gains in sample efficiency and final performance on a suite of long-duration tasks from the DMLab, Minecraft, and robotic manipulation domains.
proposals.novelty: While hierarchical RL and world models are both active areas, the novelty lies in the tight integration of a *learned* hierarchical latent dynamics model for planning, moving beyond flat models or hand-engineered skills, and leveraging video prediction as a training signal for low-level policy learning.

proposals.title: Learning Robust World Models via Self-Supervised Semantic Attention
proposals.motivation: Current world models, like Dreamer, learn dynamics from pixels but often latch onto superficial visual correlations, making them brittle to distribution shifts and adversarial perturbations. This fragility limits their real-world applicability. A robust agent must focus on semantically meaningful, object-level features to generalize across novel scenes and tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: Pretraining a visual attention mechanism with a self-supervised objective that enforces coherence of attended regions over time will yield a semantic bottleneck, which, when integrated into a world model, will produce more robust and generalizable latent dynamics.
proposals.method: We propose a two-stage framework. First, a transformer-based attention module is pretrained on large, unlabeled video datasets using a slot-attention style reconstruction loss, where each slot must reconstruct a temporally consistent image region. Second, this frozen attention module guides a recurrent world model (e.g., DreamerV3) by providing object-centric representations as input. The dynamics model is then trained with reinforcement learning, using the pretrained attention as a fixed, semantically-grounded perceptual front-end.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a method for learning world models that are significantly more robust to visual perturbations and novel scene compositions. It will also provide a more interpretable agent by explicitly revealing the object-level features guiding its decisions, advancing the field of robust model-based RL.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses auxiliary losses or object discovery within world model training, our approach decouples semantic representation learning from dynamics learning via a dedicated, pretrained attention module, explicitly enforcing temporal coherence as a self-supervised signal for robustness.

proposals.title: Inferring Non-Stationary Dynamics via Ensemble World-Model Planning with Latent Hypotheses
proposals.motivation: Real-world environments are often non-stationary and procedurally generated, presenting a fundamental challenge for model-based reinforcement learning agents. Pre-2026 methods like DreamerV3 excel in stable domains but struggle when the underlying rules or dynamics can change, as a single world model can become obsolete. This research addresses the critical need for agents that can actively infer and adapt to such shifts during a single episode.
proposals.hypothesis: An ensemble of world models, each conditioned on a distinct latent hypothesis about the environment's generative process, can enable an agent to efficiently infer non-stationary dynamics through planning. The divergence in predicted outcomes across the ensemble provides a signal for directed exploration and robust policy selection.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture with a large parallel ensemble of world models. Each model's recurrent state will be conditioned on a learnable latent code, representing a specific hypothesis about environmental rules. We will implement a planning algorithm, such as Cross-Entropy Method (CEM), to roll out imagined trajectories in parallel across all models. The agent will select actions by considering both the expected reward and the epistemic uncertainty, measured by the variance in value predictions or model accuracy across the ensemble, actively targeting states that reduce this uncertainty.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a scalable framework for continual world-model learning in procedurally generated, non-stationary settings. It will demonstrate how ensemble-based planning can be used not just for robustness, but as an active inference mechanism, leading to new benchmarks for adaptation and curiosity-driven exploration in dynamic environments.
proposals.novelty: While ensemble methods exist for robustness, conditioning each model on a distinct latent hypothesis for active rule inference during planning is novel. This creates a tight, co-adaptive loop where planning directly tests and updates beliefs about the environment's changing dynamics, moving beyond passive model averaging.

proposals.title: Multi-Agent Dreamer: Learning Interactive World Models for Decentralized Control
proposals.motivation: World models like Dreamer have enabled single agents to learn complex behaviors through latent imagination. However, many real-world tasks involve multiple interacting agents, where the dynamics are non-stationary and the joint action space is combinatorial. Scaling world models to multi-agent systems is a critical, unsolved challenge for learning robust and cooperative behaviors in partially observable environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-agent latent dynamics model, which explicitly factors agent-specific and interactive latent states, can enable sample-efficient learning of decentralized policies through joint latent imagination and prediction of other agents' behaviors.
proposals.method: We will extend the DreamerV3 architecture by introducing agent-specific latent state spaces and a shared interactive latent space, modeled by a transformer-based mixing module. Each agent's policy and value function will be trained via imagined rollouts in the latent space, using a multi-agent variant of the actor-critic loss. The model will be trained on pixel observations using a reconstruction loss and a contrastive loss to disentangle individual and interactive factors.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a scalable framework for model-based multi-agent reinforcement learning in visual domains. It will demonstrate improved sample efficiency and emergent coordination in benchmarks like multi-agent Atari, Google Research Football, and custom multi-room navigation tasks with partial observability.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has combined world models with centralized training for decentralized execution (CTDE), this proposal introduces a novel latent state factorization for modeling multi-agent interactions explicitly within the world model itself, enabling decentralized latent imagination.

ideas.title: Deconstructive Dynamics: Learning World Models via Inverse Resource Consumption
ideas.description: This research proposes training an agent to learn a general world dynamics model by solving the inverse problem of efficiently deconstructing complex structures or consuming resource networks. The agent uses two separate models: one learns the physical dynamics of how actions decompose objects and affect the environment, while a separate, decoupled model learns to predict the 'consumption value' or efficiency of different deconstruction strategies. By focusing purely on the dynamics of disassembly—understanding how components are connected and interact—without an explicit external reward prediction, the agent develops a robust, reusable understanding of object affordances and physical causality. This learned model can then be rapidly adapted to downstream construction or manipulation tasks that require the same foundational knowledge.

ideas.title: Cognitive Architecture for RL: Integrating Deliberative Planning with Habitual Control
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea of combining PlaNet's planning with a model-free policy by drawing an analogy from cognitive neuroscience. The analogous problem is how biological agents efficiently balance slow, deliberate planning with fast, habitual action. The human brain solves this with interacting model-based (prefrontal/hippocampal) and model-free (basal ganglia) systems. We adapt this solution by proposing a cognitive architecture for RL where PlaNet's model-based planner and a model-free policy network are integrated via a gating or arbitration mechanism. This mechanism, inspired by prefrontal cortex function, would decide when to rely on planned trajectories versus cached policy actions based on uncertainty, novelty, or expected value. The planner trains and provides sub-goals for the policy, while the policy's habitual responses improve real-time efficiency. This biologically-inspired integration aims to capture the best of both systems: the sample efficiency and generalization of planning with the fast execution and robustness of model-free policies, leading to improved overall performance and learning efficiency.

ideas.title: Latent Program Synthesis for Long-Horizon Tool-Crafting
ideas.description: This research direction combines program synthesis for tool-crafting with improved latent overshooting to create a novel planning agent. The agent uses a program synthesizer to generate candidate action sequences for complex crafting tasks in environments like Crafter. These candidate programs are then evaluated not in the raw environment, but within a latent dynamics model trained with an extended overshooting objective for accurate long-horizon rollouts. This synergy allows the system to reason over vastly longer and more precise sequences than pure search or model-free RL, as the learned latent model provides efficient, abstract simulation to verify program feasibility and optimize for success before costly execution.

ideas.title: Long-Horizon Latent Planning for Emergent Compositional Behaviors
ideas.description: This research direction investigates how advanced latent overshooting objectives, specifically designed for extremely long planning horizons, can scaffold the emergence of sophisticated, compositional behaviors in environments like Crafter. The core hypothesis is that behaviors such as building and farming are not just emergent phenomena to be observed, but are direct consequences of an agent's ability to form and maintain accurate latent predictions over extended sequences of interdependent sub-tasks. The study will analyze how improvements in latent temporal consistency enable the chaining of primitive actions into complex plans, using the structured complexity of emergent behaviors as both the benchmark and the guiding signal for further refining the latent model's long-horizon capabilities.

ideas.title: Apply data augmentation from contrastive learning

ideas.title: Adversarially Grounded Latent Overshooting for Stable Long-Horizon Learning
ideas.description: This approach extends latent overshooting by directly combating the divergence of long-horizon latent trajectories. A critic network is trained to differentiate between imagined multi-step latent trajectories and latent encodings of real environment trajectories, providing a learned consistency signal. The latent model is then trained not only to minimize prediction error but also to minimize the critic's ability to distinguish its long-horizon predictions, regularizing the dynamics. This adversarial grounding, combined with a horizon curriculum, enables stable training of significantly longer prediction horizons while preserving the realism of the latent state space.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-Aware Adaptive Arbitration Between Model-Based Planning and Model-Free Policy
ideas.description: This system integrates a PlaNet-style planner with a model-free policy but introduces a core arbitration module to prevent destructive interference. The arbitrator continuously evaluates the epistemic uncertainty of the world model's predictions and the confidence of the policy's value estimates. Based on these metrics, it dynamically blends the actions from both components or chooses one exclusively, ensuring the planner guides exploration in uncertain states while the policy stabilizes exploitation in well-known regions. This approach maintains sample efficiency from planning while achieving robust final performance by shielding the policy from poor model-based targets during early learning.

ideas.title: Grounded Symbolic Query (GSQ): Evaluating Emergent Abstraction from Pure Visual Input
ideas.description: This research tests whether agents trained on pure visual input in Crafter develop human-interpretable abstract representations, without using symbolic inputs for training. The agent interacts with the environment solely through pixels and actions. Separately, during evaluation phases, the agent is presented with symbolic queries about its internal state and environment. It must generate symbolic answers using a learned, grounded vocabulary, demonstrating the abstraction it has built from visual data. This approach maintains the perceptual challenge while providing a rigorous, comparable metric for the emergence and utility of symbolic reasoning grounded in vision.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Compositional Planning in a Vast, Structured Open World
ideas.description: This research direction proposes the development of a massively scaled, procedurally generated 'Mega-Crafter' environment featuring a deep hierarchy of items, crafting recipes, and interacting entities. The agent must operate in an exponentially expanded, structured action space where primitive actions (like movement and interaction) can be composed into complex skills (like 'forge a sword' or 'build a shelter'), which themselves become new macro-actions in a hierarchical planning library. The core challenge is to design a planning method that can efficiently explore and exploit this compositional action hierarchy to achieve long-horizon goals in the vast world, thereby jointly solving the problems of scaling to high-dimensional action spaces and scaling to immense environmental complexity through the learning of reusable skill abstractions.

ideas.title: Ensemble World-Model Planning in Procedurally Generated Non-Stationary Environments
ideas.description: This research direction scales ensemble planning to operate in a world where the environment is not static but procedurally generated and dynamically changing. The key innovation is that each model in the large parallel ensemble is conditioned on a different hypothesis or latent code about the environment's generative rules and potential change dynamics. The system then plans trajectories in parallel across this ensemble of 'possible worlds,' using the divergence in plan success and model accuracy to actively infer the true underlying environmental dynamics and to guide exploration towards regions that reduce this epistemic uncertainty. This creates a co-adaptive loop where planning improves the world model, and an improved world model directs more robust and curious planning.

ideas.title: Symbolic Scaffold: Co-training Abstract Reasoning and Grounded Execution in Crafter
ideas.description: This approach constructs a minimal symbolic representation learned directly from agent experience in the original Crafter environment. An automated process abstracts successful trajectories into a predicate-based planning domain. The core system then operates in a dual-loop: a high-level planner reasons over the symbolic scaffold to propose subgoals and plans, while a low-level controller executes these plans in the pixel-based environment. This isolates reasoning challenges by providing an abstract space for search, but crucially tests the grounding and robustness of that reasoning by requiring execution in the original, rich observational space, preventing a disconnect from the core challenges.

ideas.title: Self-Supervised Semantic Attention for Robust Visual Dynamics Learning
ideas.description: This approach introduces a two-stage training framework where the visual attention mechanism is first pretrained using a self-supervised objective that enforces attention to semantically coherent regions without using task-specific labels. The pretrained attention module then guides the dynamics learning model, focusing it on stable, object-level features rather than superficial correlations. This results in a more robust and interpretable system with improved generalization to novel scenes and adversarial perturbations.

ideas.title: Symbolic Emergence: Studying the Abstract Primitives of Complex Agent Behaviors
ideas.description: This research creates a minimal, symbolic, and fully observable testbed where the abstract prerequisites for behaviors like building and farming are the core mechanics. By stripping away perceptual complexity, the work isolates the fundamental reasoning challenges—such as long-horizon planning with sparse rewards, managing interdependent sub-goals, and understanding causal chains—that underpin sophisticated emergence. This allows for formal analysis of the cognitive architectures and learning algorithms that can or cannot bootstrap these behaviors from basic primitives. The resulting framework provides a rigorous, interpretable benchmark for studying how complex capabilities can emerge from simpler modules in a controlled setting.

ideas.title: Apply Meta-Learning with a Task-Specific Modulation Network for Fast Dynamics Adaptation
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from computational immunology, where a general immune system model is combined with a fast-adapting component to target new pathogens, we adapt this solution to meta-learning for latent dynamics. The core idea is to separate the learning architecture into a fixed, meta-learned base dynamics model that encodes general physical principles and a small, task-specific modulation network. This modulation network, which can be a hypernetwork or a set of conditioning layers, is quickly adapted from a few trajectories of a new task. It then generates parameters (e.g., shifts, scales, or attention masks) that modulate the forward passes of the base model, allowing for rapid and data-efficient adaptation to new but related dynamical systems without catastrophic forgetting of the foundational knowledge.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Multi-Agent Skill Learning and Coordination in Crafter
ideas.description: This research investigates hierarchical reinforcement learning (HRL) within a multi-agent Crafter environment, where agents must learn and coordinate temporally extended macro-actions (skills) to survive and thrive collectively. The core novelty lies in studying how a population of agents can discover, specialize in, and share diverse skill hierarchies, moving beyond individual skill learning to a collaborative or competitive skill economy. This framework allows us to explore emergent phenomena like skill markets, division of labor, and the co-evolution of complementary skill hierarchies, addressing the combined challenges of long-horizon planning and multi-agent interaction in a complex, open-ended world.

ideas.title: Procedural Visual Distractor Generation for Robust Skill Acquisition
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a procedurally generated Crafter environment where the core rules and goals change, and the visual distractors are procedurally generated as an integral part of the world generation process. This prevents the agent from relying on static visual cues or memorizing a fixed set of irrelevant details, as both the task and the noise are novel each episode. The synergy forces the agent to learn a meta-skill of dynamically distinguishing between task-critical visual elements and procedurally generated visual clutter, leading to more robust and general visual grounding. The ultimate test is transferring the agent to entirely new, unseen environments with different visual styles and distractor types to evaluate true generalization.

ideas.title: Curriculum-Driven Procedural Generation with a Relevance and Solvability Guarantee
ideas.description: This system replaces open-ended procedural generation with a curriculum-based generator controlled by a learned oracle. The oracle uses the agent's current proficiency and a model of core game mechanics to propose new environment configurations that are provably solvable but require the agent to extend its known skill set. This creates a targeted, adversarial training curriculum where the world changes dynamically, but always in a way that forces robust skill acquisition without creating impossible or trivial tasks, leading to agents that generalize reliably within a well-defined capability envelope.

ideas.title: Apply Attentional Spotlighting from Neuroscience to Filter Visual Distractors in Agent Tasks
ideas.description: This research idea adapts the solution to visual noise filtering from cognitive neuroscience—specifically, the 'attentional spotlight' mechanism of human selective attention—to the problem of agent performance in environments with heavy visual distractors. The analogous solution involves top-down modulation from higher-order brain regions that suppress irrelevant early visual processing. The adapted idea is to design an agent architecture where a trainable, goal-directed attention module learns to apply a dynamic spotlight or mask over the raw visual input or early feature maps. This module would be trained via reinforcement or supervised learning to amplify features critical for the current task (e.g., an object to manipulate) while attenuating complex but irrelevant background details, thereby improving robustness and sample efficiency in visually noisy environments.

ideas.title: Adapting Drug Candidate Screening to Latent Space Planning via Bayesian Optimization
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational chemistry and drug discovery, where Bayesian optimization efficiently navigates high-dimensional molecular spaces to find optimal compounds. The analogous problem is the expensive screening of a vast, structured search space for a high-performing candidate. In the research context of latent space planning, we treat the planner's latent action space as the search domain and the expected return of a plan as the black-box function to maximize. By employing a Gaussian process to model this reward function and using an acquisition function (like Expected Improvement) to guide query selection, the planner can sequentially propose and test compact latent plans. This approach balances exploring novel strategies and exploiting known successful ones, enabling data-efficient and effective planning by minimizing the number of costly trajectory rollouts needed to discover high-reward action sequences.

ideas.title: Competence-Scaled World Generation with Automated Curriculum
ideas.description: This research system creates a complex, open-ended environment where world size and the availability of items, recipes, and threats are not fixed but are generated in proportion to a learned measure of agent competence. An automated curriculum manager monitors the agent's skill acquisition and dynamically unlocks new terrain, crafting trees, and challenges, while also providing optional guided subgoals to bootstrap exploration in the expanded possibility space. This ensures that the agent is continuously exposed to a 'just-manageable' level of complexity, directly tackling the exploration bottleneck inherent in naive scaling. The core experiment measures how this competence-aware scaling affects the rate of skill acquisition and final asymptotic performance compared to static, large-scale environments.

ideas.title: Develop agents with a hierarchical curriculum of skill modules for comprehensive achievement mastery
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from developmental psychology and educational theory, which solves the problem of teaching complex, cumulative skill sets through structured, hierarchical curricula. The analogous solution involves decomposing an ultimate goal into foundational sub-skills, teaching them in a progressive prerequisite order, and using scaffolding that fades with competence. Adapted to the research context, this suggests developing agents not as monolithic learners but through a hierarchical curriculum of skill modules. The agent would first robustly master foundational skill modules (e.g., navigation, resource collection, basic combat) in targeted training environments. A higher-level meta-controller would then be trained to sequence and compose these reliable skill modules to execute the long-term reasoning and deep exploration necessary to unlock all Crafter achievements, effectively providing a learning scaffold from simple to comprehensive mastery.

ideas.title: Intrinsic Motivation via Behavioral Homeostasis for Crafter
ideas.description: This research advances intrinsic motivation by drawing an analogy from systems physiology, specifically the concept of homeostasis. In biology, homeostasis maintains internal stability through feedback loops that trigger corrective behaviors. The analogous solution is to model an agent's internal state with a set of drifting motivational variables (e.g., curiosity, competence), each with a homeostatic setpoint. The adapted idea is to generate intrinsic reward based on actions that reduce the error between the current state and these setpoints. This creates a dynamic, self-regulating drive that continuously propels the unsupervised agent to explore new territories and master complex skills in the Crafter benchmark to satisfy its shifting internal needs, thereby improving performance scores.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Reward Shaping: From Survival to Cultural Complexity via Ecological Succession
ideas.description: This idea adapts the ecological concept of succession to scale reinforcement learning objectives in Crafter from basic survival to complex societal or narrative generation. The analogous solution from ecology is the staged, conditional process of primary succession, where simple organisms pave the way for increasingly complex ecosystems. This is adapted by designing a hierarchical, state-contingent reward curriculum: the agent must first satisfy 'pioneer' survival rewards, which alters the environment state and activates a new reward layer for 'intermediate' societal-building tasks, culminating in a final 'climax' layer rewarding the generation of persistent narratives or cultural artifacts. The scaling is dual, involving both the complexity of the objective and the conditional structure of the reward function itself.

ideas.title: Bayesian Optimization for Efficient Multi-Ability Benchmarking in Crafter
ideas.description: This research direction uses Bayesian optimization (BO) to guide the search for policies in a latent skill or plan space, with the explicit objective of efficiently evaluating and discovering a broad spectrum of general abilities within the Crafter benchmark. Instead of random or exhaustive evaluation, BO's surrogate model learns from prior rollouts to propose policy parameters most likely to yield previously unobserved capabilities or skill combinations. This turns Crafter into an active benchmarking platform that can systematically map the frontier of an agent's learnable abilities, providing a quantitative measure of generality and a targeted curriculum for improvement.

ideas.title: Multimodal Sensory Augmentation for Embodied AI via Symbolic-Language Interfaces
ideas.description: This idea challenges the visual-input-only paradigm in embodied AI by drawing an analogy from HCI and accessibility technology, where visual-only interfaces are transformed into multimodal experiences for users with visual impairments. The analogous solution involves creating parallel information channels (e.g., auditory via screen readers, tactile via refreshable Braille) that convey the same underlying system state. Adapted to the Crafter environment, this means augmenting the raw pixel input with a synchronous, symbolic or natural language interface that provides structured descriptions of critical game state—such as inventory contents, resource locations, health status, and current objectives. The research would investigate how agents can learn to attend to and fuse this optional language-based 'accessibility layer' with visual features, testing whether this multimodal input breaks reliance on pixel-only pattern recognition, accelerates the learning of complex skills, and enables more robust hierarchical planning by providing direct access to abstract state variables.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Competence-Aware Curiosity for Robust Unsupervised Skill Discovery in Crafter
ideas.description: This adversarially improved idea proposes a hierarchical intrinsic motivation system designed to overcome the derailment and distraction pitfalls of naive curiosity. It uses a meta-learning layer to direct exploration towards learnable and skill-relevant dynamics, filtering out noisy or trivial novelties. The lower level employs competence-based intrinsic rewards that incentivize the acquisition of novel, reliable action-effect relationships (skills) rather than mere observation novelty. This results in a more robust, directed exploration process that systematically builds a repertoire of useful skills and abstract knowledge in the Crafter environment.

ideas.title: Robust Program Synthesis for Tool Crafting via Uncertainty-Aware World Models
ideas.description: This system combines symbolic program synthesis for structured planning with a learned world model that captures the stochastic dynamics of Crafter. The synthesizer proposes candidate action sequences for tool crafting, while the world model evaluates each step under the current game state, estimating success probabilities and potential hazards. The planner selects and executes the sequence with the highest expected robustness, monitored by the world model for deviations. If the predicted uncertainty exceeds a threshold or execution fails, the model triggers re-synthesis with updated constraints, creating a closed-loop, adaptive crafting strategy.

ideas.title: Adaptive Curriculum for Robust Skill Acquisition via Procedural World Generation
ideas.description: This idea challenges the need for a single, static environment by drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology's Red Queen Hypothesis. The analogous solution is the constant, adaptive pressure exerted by a changing ecosystem, which drives species to develop robust and generalist traits. Adapted to Crafter, this involves creating a procedurally generated environment that dynamically changes not randomly, but in direct response to the agent's learned capabilities—for example, by increasing environmental complexity, introducing novel obstacles, or shifting resource dependencies as the agent masters current tasks. This creates a perpetual 'arms race' between the agent and its world, forcing the learning of foundational, transferable skills essential for generalization, rather than memorization of a fixed environment.

ideas.title: Model Reduction via Balanced Truncation for Embedded World Models
ideas.description: This research adapts the model order reduction technique of 'balanced truncation' from control theory to downscale the PlaNet model for embedded systems. The analogous solution involves analyzing complex dynamical systems to retain only the most critical states. Adapted to this context, the method would profile the learned latent dynamics of PlaNet, apply a metric for the controllability and observability of each latent dimension, and systematically prune the least significant dimensions. The result is a formally simplified, fixed-complexity world model that maintains core predictive power for planning, enabling real-time inference on resource-constrained embedded devices for control tasks, analogous to deploying reduced-order controllers in engineering systems.

ideas.title: Structured Long-Memory Latent Dynamics via Tractable Stochastic Processes
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond simply removing the Markov assumption by explicitly modeling the latent state with a tractable, structured stochastic process designed to capture long-range dependencies. Instead of an unbounded history, the latent dynamics are governed by a parameterized model with built-in long memory, such as a fractional Gaussian process or a high-order linear system with regularized decay. This structure enables the development of efficient variational inference algorithms and learning objectives that remain computationally feasible. The result is a model that robustly handles long-term dependencies without collapsing into intractability, providing a principled middle ground between restrictive Markov models and unstructured, infinite-history models.

ideas.title: Safe-by-Construction Lightweight World Models with Verified Safety Filters
ideas.description: This research develops a resource-efficient world model for embedded control that is robust to the model's inevitable inaccuracies. The core innovation is a two-component architecture: a small, learned recurrent model for task performance is paired with an extremely lightweight, formally verified safety monitor. The safety monitor, derived from simplified physics or conservative abstractions of the environment, continuously validates and can override the learned model's proposed actions in real-time. This ensures that the system never executes actions known to lead to catastrophic states, making the downscaled model viable for real-world deployment.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-Aware Meta-Learning with Fallback for Robust Dynamics Adaptation
ideas.description: This system combines meta-learning for fast adaptation with a robust safety mechanism for handling out-of-distribution tasks. It employs an ensemble of meta-learned models to provide both a rapid initial dynamics estimate and a reliable measure of epistemic uncertainty upon encountering a new task. If the uncertainty is low, standard meta-test adaptation proceeds; if high, the system switches to a safe exploration mode guided by uncertainty reduction, collecting data to perform online Bayesian model correction. This ensures robust performance across both in-distribution and novel OOD tasks, preventing catastrophic failures.

ideas.title: Multi-Scale Latent Overshooting with Hierarchical Error Correction
ideas.description: This idea extends latent overshooting by drawing an analogy from Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP), where multi-scale modeling and ensemble forecasting are used to improve long-term forecast accuracy. The analogous solution involves running weather models at different spatial/temporal resolutions (coarse for stability, fine for local correction) and using ensemble averages to reduce error compounding. Adapted to latent dynamics models, this leads to a novel training objective: a hierarchical latent overshooting scheme. Multiple latent pathways operate at different temporal granularities, with a coarse pathway trained for long-horizon consistency and finer pathways for local accuracy. Additionally, an ensemble of latent rollouts with varied initial perturbations is generated during training, and their predictions are aggregated (e.g., via a learned weighting or simple average) to define a robust, multi-horizon overshooting loss that mitigates compounding error and improves planning over extended futures.

ideas.title: Automated Curriculum Generation for Hierarchical Symbolic Reasoning in a Minimal Crafter
ideas.description: This research creates a minimal, symbolic, and fully observable version of Crafter to serve as a testbed for automated curriculum learning aimed at hierarchical reasoning. The curriculum generator manipulates the environment's symbolic rules and available action primitives to gradually introduce necessary subgoals and compositional dependencies, such as requiring tool-making before complex crafting. This allows for the systematic study of how agents can learn to form and execute multi-step plans from a curriculum of increasingly complex symbolic puzzles, providing clear insights into the acquisition of structured knowledge and long-horizon reasoning.

ideas.title: Ensemble Planning via Distributed Computing and Asynchronous Consensus
ideas.description: This idea scales the planner by using a massive ensemble of models to plan trajectories in parallel, drawing an analogy from distributed fault-tolerant computing. The analogous solution uses redundant, parallel nodes and asynchronous consensus algorithms to achieve reliable agreement despite failures. Adapted to planning, a distributed ensemble of planning models generates trajectories concurrently on parallel hardware. A dedicated consensus layer, inspired by protocols like PBFT, operates asynchronously to compare, vote on, and merge trajectory segments from the ensemble, dynamically focusing computational resources on the most promising and agreed-upon future paths. This creates a scalable, robust planning system that can efficiently explore a vast action space by leveraging parallel computation and principled aggregation.

ideas.title: Minimal Symbolic Arena for Multi-Agent Protocol Emergence
ideas.description: This research creates a minimal, fully symbolic version of the Crafter environment designed for multiple agents. The simplified, observable state space isolates the core challenges of planning, negotiation, and decentralized coordination under resource constraints. By removing pixel-based perception, the work focuses exclusively on how agents can develop communication protocols and collaborative strategies from first principles to achieve shared or competing survival goals. This enables controlled experiments in emergent multi-agent reasoning that are intractable in the complex, noisy original environment.

ideas.title: Structured Social Curriculum Learning with Disentangled Multi-Agent Evaluation in Crafter
ideas.description: This research proposes a methodical framework for studying multi-agent interactions in Crafter by deliberately constraining and structuring the learning problem to isolate social intelligence. It employs a curriculum starting with a single learning agent interacting with frozen, pre-trained partners, focusing purely on learning cooperative and competitive protocols. The framework then progressively introduces more adaptive partners and joint training scenarios. A core innovation is a disentangled evaluation suite that rigorously separates an agent's individual survival proficiency from its social capabilities, enabling clear attribution of performance to specific interaction mechanisms and yielding interpretable, reproducible insights into emergent multi-agent behavior.

ideas.title: Adversarial Generation of Semantically Plausible Visual Distractors
ideas.description: This research tests agent robustness against active, semantically meaningful visual deception, not just passive noise. An adversarial network dynamically generates contextually plausible but task-irrelevant objects and textures within the environment, optimized to exploit the agent's known or learned attention weaknesses. The agent is then trained in this adversarial loop, forcing it to develop grounding in core task semantics and robust visual reasoning that resists deceptive cues. This creates a stronger test of generalization and attentional robustness compared to static or random visual noise.

ideas.title: Procedural Milestone Curriculum for Grounded Emergent Complexity in Crafter
ideas.description: This research introduces a framework that procedurally generates a curriculum of concrete, evaluable milestones that scaffold from basic survival to societal and narrative complexity within the Crafter environment. The system defines a grammar of 'societal structures' (e.g., multi-room settlements with functional zones) and 'narrative quests' (e.g., sequences of crafted items and NPC interactions with causal logic), providing automated, quantifiable reward signals for achieving them. The agent learns through a hybrid reward combining this curriculum with intrinsic motivation for novelty and skill acquisition, ensuring objectives are learnable and non-degenerate while promoting open-ended, grounded complexity.

ideas.title: A Spectral Metric for Skill Acquisition: Replacing Binary Achievements with Continuous Progress Vectors
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that achievement in learning (e.g., in AI agents or educational assessment) must be evaluated with discrete, binary metrics. Drawing an analogy from spectroscopy in materials science, where material composition is analyzed via continuous spectral signatures rather than single binary indicators, we propose a novel evaluation framework. The analogous solution uses the full shape and features of a spectrum as a fingerprint. Adapted to this research context, an agent's progress toward a skill or goal is not a single unlock but a multi-dimensional 'progress vector' or 'behavioral spectrum.' This vector is constructed from performance features across a designed suite of related tasks, environmental variations, or subtask decompositions. The metric measures the continuous shift of this vector from a novice baseline toward an expert target signature, thereby quantifying partial progress, compositional understanding, and the specific facets of a skill that have been acquired.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Action Space Decomposition via Protein Folding Analogy
ideas.description: This research seed proposes extending a planning method to domains with very high-dimensional or structured action spaces by drawing an analogy from computational biology's solution to protein structure prediction. The analogous problem is navigating the astronomically large conformational space of a protein, which is solved hierarchically (e.g., in Rosetta) by assembling known stable local fragments at a coarse level before refining atomic details. The adapted idea is to decompose a complex action space into a hierarchy of abstract, reusable 'action motifs' or sub-policies. Planning would first operate in this reduced motif space to find a feasible coarse plan. This coarse plan then serves as a scaffold, with detailed actions generated by refining each motif through local optimization or learned generative models, thereby making planning in very high-dimensional spaces tractable.

ideas.title: Adapting Non-Markovian Latent States via Hidden Semi-Markov Models from Computational Biology
ideas.description: This idea proposes to relax the Markov assumption in latent sequential models by drawing an analogy from computational biology's solution to gene finding. In that field, Hidden Semi-Markov Models (HSMMs) were introduced to overcome the limitations of HMMs, which could not model the known, non-geometric length distributions of exons and introns. An HSMM explicitly models the duration a system stays in a hidden state, separating the state transition dynamics from the state residence time. Adapting this solution, we propose a latent variable model where each discrete latent state has an associated explicit duration distribution (e.g., Poisson, Negative Binomial). The model operates in segments: it samples a latent state and its duration, then generates the corresponding block of observations before transitioning to the next latent state. This structure breaks the Markovian 'one-step' dependency, allowing the current latent context to depend on a longer history defined by the state's duration, thereby more naturally handling longer-term dependencies and multi-scale temporal patterns in sequential data.

ideas.title: Emergent Social Structures in a Scaled, Persistent Multi-Agent Crafter World
ideas.description: This research direction creates a massively scaled-up, persistent version of Crafter designed for a population of long-lived AI agents. The expanded environment features diverse biomes, rare resources, complex crafting trees, and dynamic threats that no single agent can master or control alone. This forces agents to interact over extended timescales, leading to the emergence of sophisticated social behaviors like division of labor, resource trading, cooperative defense, and possibly competition or conflict over territory. The core investigation is how these social structures and agent roles evolve from first principles of survival in a complex world, providing a new benchmark for emergent multi-agent coordination and social intelligence.

ideas.title: Narrative and Societal Flourishing via Compositional Progress Metrics
ideas.description: This research direction proposes replacing Crafter's discrete achievement unlocks with a continuous, compositional progress metric that directly quantifies the richness of emergent narratives and the complexity of agent-constructed societies. The agent's objective is scaled from survival to actively generating coherent storylines and stable multi-agent social structures, with the novel metric providing dense, shaped reward signals for partial progress toward these abstract goals. This enables the learning of policies that strategically build foundational elements (e.g., resources, tools, relationships) not just as ends, but as compositional pieces for higher-order flourishing. The synergy lies in using the fine-grained progress measurement to make the training of notoriously sparse and long-horizon objectives like storytelling and societal development tractable and optimizable.

ideas.title: Ecosystem-Inspired Multi-Agent Crafter: Modeling Niche Construction and Trophic Interactions
ideas.description: This idea inverts the single-agent focus in Crafter by drawing an analogy from Theoretical Ecology. Ecologists solve the problem of understanding complex biological communities by modeling interacting species with specialized niches and analyzing their dynamics through concepts like predator-prey cycles and niche construction. Adapted to Crafter, this proposes designing a heterogeneous multi-agent system with specialized agent 'species' (e.g., miners, farmers, fighters) to study emergent ecological dynamics like symbiosis, competition, and the formation of stable agent 'food webs' within the survival setting, challenging the core assumption that the environment must be mastered by a solo, generalist intelligence.

ideas.title: Applying Ecological Succession Theory to Model the Emergence of Complex Agent Behaviors
ideas.description: This research adapts the concept of ecological succession to analyze emergent complex behaviors in Crafter. In ecology, simple pioneer species facilitate environmental changes that allow for increasingly complex communities. The analogous solution involves modeling organism-environment feedback loops and succession thresholds. Adapted to this context, the research will analyze the conditions for sophisticated emergent behaviors like building and farming by treating the agent's early actions as 'pioneer behaviors' that alter the in-game environmental state. The goal is to map the sequence of behavioral niches and identify the critical environmental thresholds and reinforcement pathways that must be met for stable, high-level compound behaviors to succeed simpler ones, providing a predictive model for the emergence of complexity.

ideas.title: Modular Ecosystem Scaffolding for AI Agent Scaling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the ecological scaffolding method to the problem of scaling environment complexity for AI agents. The analogous problem in conservation biology is rebuilding a large, complex ecosystem without causing collapse. The solution is a modular, sequential approach starting with a stable foundational core. Adapted here, the research involves creating a larger, more complex version of Crafter not as a monolithic environment, but as a series of layered, interoperable modules. A simple core 'biome' with basic items and threats is established first. Once an agent masters this, new modules—each adding specific clusters of items, recipes, or advanced threats—are activated or connected, scaling the environment's complexity in a controlled, stable manner. This allows systematic testing of how agents scale their capabilities in response to graduated increases in environmental richness and danger.

ideas.title: Canonical Difficulty Spectra for Anchored Partial Progress Evaluation
ideas.description: This approach defines progress not by a single continuous score but by performance across a structured spectrum of task variants. A generator creates a canonical set of environment or problem instances with rigorously quantified difficulty parameters (e.g., solution length, noise level, constraint density). An agent's partial achievement is then measured as a profile or frontier across this spectrum, such as the hardest solved variant or a distribution of success rates. This provides a reproducible, multi-dimensional, and objective measure of partial capability that is anchored in concrete task properties, not arbitrary scoring functions.
